Infertility","short_name":"MaleInfertility","scope_description":"
The Male Infertility GCEP will evaluate and curate genes associated with monogenic causes of quantitative and qualitative sperm defects, encompassing azoospermia, oligozoospermia, sperm morphological and functional abnormalities. These disorders represent a major cause of male infertility, which affects approximately 7% of men worldwide and contributes to up to half of all cases of couple infertility. The panel will initially focus on conditions where the primary phenotype involves impaired spermatogenesis or sperm function. Genes implicated in syndromic conditions will be included only when the male infertility phenotype is a consistent and clinically meaningful component. A dedicated Male Infertility GCEP is urgently needed because of the growing clinical demand for genetic evaluation in men with severe sperm defects, and the recent expansion of research discoveries in this field. Many genes linked to male infertility have been identified in small family studies or from isolated case reports, and lack standardized assessment of clinical validity. In addition, there is currently no consensus list of curated genes to guide clinical testing or variant interpretation in male infertility. Establishing a structured gene curation effort will support accurate diagnosis, genetic counseling, and reproductive decision-making for affected individuals and families, while also identifying knowledge gaps and opportunities for future research. The panel includes experts from around the world in reproductive genetics, urology, andrology, mouse models, molecular diagnostics, and bioinformatics, ensuring multidisciplinary oversight throughout the curation process. Gene Triage Approach: We recognize that a number of our genes are either in scope or have already been published by other GCEPs, particularly the Motile Ciliopathy GCEP. For those genes in scope but not yet started by other GCEPs (n=12), we will collaborate to determine the best approach forward, following all applicable guidance including lumping and splitting. For those genes already started or published for a relevant disease (n=10), we will briefly review to determine if any additional context/evidence related to male infertility may be necessary, and if so work with the relevant GCEP to broker a secondary contribution. There are at least two genes on our list that have been published by another GCEP for a seemingly unrelated disease term; here we will follow lumping/splitting guidance to determine if a new curation is warranted. A single gene on our list was listed as being in-scope for a non-GCEP in the GeneTracker (Counsyl and UNC Biocuration core); we will proceed with this curation as the first GCEP to formally evaluate it. The rest of our genes (n=45) are not in scope for any other GCEP, and we will focus our initial efforts on these. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:42:11","data":{"uuid":"86af9d32-9e14-43de-b2a1-01acd33b2d96","name":"RYR2","description":"Pathogenic variants in RYR2 are associated with autosomal dominant Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (CPVT) (ClinGen Definitive classification), a serious and life-threatening disease that primarily manifests in the first and second decades of life with a median age of onset between 7 and 10 years. CPVT is characterized by cardiac arrhythmia in response to physical or emotional stress, which may manifest as syncope, sudden cardiac arrest (SCA), or sudden cardiac death (SCD). RYR2 variants account for the majority of monogenic CPVT cases (~50%), with six other genes having at least moderate evidence of an association in ClinGen gene-disease curation. In CPVT, the abnormal ryanodine receptor 2 (RYR2) channel remains open more frequently (with increased sensitivity to caffeine in vitro), making the channel more prone to aberrant diastolic Ca²⁺ leak, leading to potentially life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. Distinct variants in RYR2 have been reported in association with another phenotype, described as Calcium Release Deficiency Syndrome (CRDS). This gene-disease relationship has not been formally curated by ClinGen as yet. Implicated variants lead to expression of an RYR2 protein with decreased sensitivity to caffeine in vitro, also leading to cardiac arrhythmia but without hallmark features of CPVT. There is no evidence that RYR2 is haploinsufficient. At present, many RYR2 variants are classified as variants of uncertain significance (VUS) or assigned different causality of the same mutation from one CLIA/CAP lab to the next despite using the same guideline framework for interpretation, in the absence of gene-specific rule specifications. The RYR2 variant expert panel (VCEP) will improve the interpretation of RYR2 variants by (1) defining criteria for specific classification of RYR2 variants based on American College of Medical Genetics (ACMG)/Association for Molecular Pathology (AMP) guidelines for sequence variant interpretation, (2) reviewing reported RYR2 variants in the public domain and providing expert interpretation and classification based on the expert-approved criteria and (3) sharing these interpretations publicly through submission to The *RYR2* gene encodes the cardiac ryanodine receptor, which plays a critical role in excitation-contraction coupling though release of calcium ions from cardiac intracellular calcium stores (the sarcomplasmic reticulum). It is a large gene, with 105 exons, encoding a protein of ~5000 amino acids. Pathogenic variants in RYR2 are associated with autosomal dominant Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (CPVT) (ClinGen Definitive classification), a serious and life-threatening disease that primarily manifests in the first and second decades of life with a median age of onset between 7 and 10 years. CPVT is characterized by cardiac arrhythmia in response to physical or emotional stress, which may manifest as syncope, sudden cardiac arrest (SCA), or sudden cardiac death (SCD). RYR2 variants account for the majority of monogenic CPVT cases (~50%), with six other genes having at least moderate evidence of an association in ClinGen gene-disease curation. In CPVT, the abnormal ryanodine receptor 2 (RYR2) channel remains open more frequently (with increased sensitivity to caffeine in vitro), making the channel more prone to aberrant diastolic Ca²⁺ leak, leading to potentially life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. Distinct variants in RYR2 have been reported in association with another phenotype, described as Calcium Release Deficiency Syndrome (CRDS). This gene-disease relationship has not been formally curated by ClinGen as yet. Implicated variants lead to expression of an RYR2 protein with decreased sensitivity to caffeine in vitro, also leading to cardiac arrhythmia but without hallmark features of CPVT. There is no evidence that RYR2 is haploinsufficient. At present, many RYR2 variants are classified as variants of uncertain significance (VUS) or assigned different causality of the same mutation from one CLIA/CAP lab to the next despite using the same guideline framework for interpretation, in the absence of gene-specific rule specifications. The RYR2 variant expert panel (VCEP) will improve the interpretation of RYR2 variants by (1) defining criteria for specific classification of RYR2 variants based on American College of Medical Genetics (ACMG)/Association for Molecular Pathology (AMP) guidelines for sequence variant interpretation, (2) reviewing reported RYR2 variants in the public domain and providing expert interpretation and classification based on the expert-approved criteria and (3) sharing these interpretations publicly through submission to ClinVar.

Our Variant Curation Expert Panel (VCEP) consists of cardiologists and electrophysiologists, along with experts in cardiovascular genetics and variant interpretation. All individuals are knowledgeable of the ACMG/AMP Guidelines for Sequence Interpretation. Co-chair Andrew Landstrom, MD, PhD is a pediatric cardiologist with expertise in pediatric electrophysiology, pediatric cardiomyopathy and heart failure, and cardiovascular genetics. He leads a basic science lab exploring the genetic and cellular causes of cardiac channelopathies, cardiomyopathies, and congenital heart disease. While Dr. Landstrom does not signoff on genetic testing results at the time of genetic testing, he re-interprets genetic testing company findings and specializes in resolving VUSs, and, on rare occasion, demote variants from diagnostic (that then later are proven to not be diagnostic). He has a 7 year history of doing this in his CV genetics clinic. He is also a member of the Cardiovascular CDWG, Hereditary Cardiovascular Disease GCEP, Potassium Channel Arrhythmia VCEP, and Sodium and Calcium Channel Arrhythmia VCEP. Co-Chair James Ware, PhD, MBBChir is a Professor of Cardiovascular and Genomic Medicine at Imperial College London, MRC Investigator at the MRC Laboratory of Medical Sciences, and Honorary Consultant Cardiologist at Royal Brompton and Harefield Hospitals, and at Imperial College Healthcare. He also has a visiting position at the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard. James’ overarching research aims are to understand the impact of genetic variation on the heart and circulation, and to use genome information to improve patient care. In addition to his own research programme, James co-leads the Cardiovascular Theme of the NIHR Imperial Biomedical Research Centre, and is Director of the Rare Disease Research UK Cardiovascular Initiative, a national programme supported by the NIHR and MRC. He is a clinical advisor to Cardiomyopathy UK, and scientific advisor to a number of pharmaceutical and biotech companies. In the clinic, James specializes in the diagnosis and management of inherited cardiac conditions, including cardiomyopathies and inherited arrhythmia syndromes. He has contributed to several ClinGen GCEPs and VCEPs for inherited cardiovascular conditions, and is on the steering committee for the Gene Curation Coalition. Wayne Chen, PhD is a professor in the Department of Physiology and Pharmacology at the University of Calgary. Dr. Chen’s research is directed towards understanding the structure/function relationships and the roles of store calcium release channels (ryanodine receptors, RyRs, and inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptors, IP3Rs) in cardiac and neuronal functions and dysfunctions. Valeria Novelli, PhD, is a Clinical Research Scientist and Chair of the Cardiogenetic Program at the Centro Cardiologico Monzino in Milan. She earned her PhD in Molecular Medicine from the University of Milan in 2009, developing her thesis project at the Joslin Diabetes Center in Boston. Following this, she joined the Cardiovascular Genetics Program at NYU Langone Medical Center as a postdoctoral researcher under the leadership of Dr. Silvia Priori, where she focused on the identification of novel genes associated with sudden cardiac death. In 2012, Dr. Novelli moved to Pavia to continue her research on ion channel proteins and subsequently completed her residency in Medical Genetics at the Catholic University in Rome in 2019. Since 2015, she has been an active member of the Cardiovascular Clinical Domain Working Group (CDWG), serving as Chair of the Potassium Channel Arrhythmia Variant Curation Expert Panel (VCEP), and as an expert member of the Sodium and Calcium Channel Arrhythmia VCEPs. From 2015, Dr. Novelli regularly sign out reports for genetic testing regarding Cardiomyopathies and, Primary Arrhythmia Diseases, including *RyR2* gene variants. Filip Van Petegem, PhD is a professor in the Biochemistry and Molecular Biology department of the Life Sciences Institute, University of British Columbia. Filip Van Petegem obtained his PhD in Biochemistry at Ghent University (Belgium) in 2002, where he was trained as an X-ray crystallographer. He then moved to UCSF for postdoctoral studies, investigating the structure and function of voltage-gated calcium channels. In 2007, he joined the Dept. of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology at UBC, where he was promoted to Full Professor in 2017. His lab uses electrophysiology and structural biology methods to study ion channels. Expertise with RyR2 includes cryo-EM structures and planar lipid bilayer electrophysiology of Ryanodine Receptors in wild-type and disease mutant forms. Advises on putative pathogenicity on RyR2 variants by mapping them on 3D structures and analyzing position, chemical environment, and binding sites for auxiliary proteins and small molecules. Kate Thomson, PhD, BSc, FRCPath is a Principal Clinical Scientist in the Oxford Medical Genetics Laboratory and the lead scientist for inherited cardiac conditions. Since completing her DPhil with Prof Hugh Watkins in 2018, she has held an Honorary post at the Division of Cardiovascular Medicine, University of Oxford, and maintains a dual clinical and research role. Kate’s research focuses on understanding the genetic basis of inherited cardiac conditions and using genome information to improve results interpretation in a clinical setting. She works with collaborators in the UK and internationally and is currently a co-investigator on the Rare Disease Research UK (RDRUK) Cardiovascular Initiative and a member of the Cure Heart team. She is also a member of the Cardiomyopathy VCEP. Arthur A.M. Wilde (male), Cardiologist/Electrophysiologist, is an expert in genetic heart disease. From the Netherlands Heart Association he was awarded a grant as Clinical Established Investigator for five years. Between 2003 and 2020 he served as head of the Department of Clinical and Experimental Cardiology (Academic Medical Centre) and between 2012 and 2019 as Chair of the Heart Centre Division, AMC. Since 2019 he is co-chair of the Amsterdam Cardiovascular Science Institute. In 2011 he was appointed as member of the Dutch Academy of Science and in 2012 he received the Distinguished Investigator award of the Heart Rhythm Society and in 2023 the gold medal of the European Society of Cardiology. Since 2017 he leads the European Reference Network GUARD-Heart (The ERN for rare cardiac diseases). He has established the international CPVT registry, serving as an important source for improvement of care for CPVT patients. He has contributed to several ClinGen GCEPs for inherited cardiovascular conditions among which is CPVT. Brittany Thomas, CGC, MS, biocurator. Brittany has over 20 years of experience in variant interpretation and reporting for rare disease and cancer. As a laboratory genetic counselor, first in the molecular genetics lab at Mayo Clinic and then as a staff genetic counselor and manager in the clinical laboratory at Illumina, Inc, she has curated variants for single gene and large molecular gene panels for indications including hereditary colorectal and breast cancer, renal diseases, congenital neurodevelopmental disorders and others. She specifically helped launch Mayo's first germline CRC panel and developed the interpretive and reporting services for somatic variants supporting the lab's companion diagnostic testing services. At Illumina, Brittany managed a team of analysts who curated genes and variants identified through whole genome sequencing for rare disease indications (neurologic, cardiovascular, neurodevelopmental, endocrine, immunologic, etc) utilizing the ACMG and ClinGen frameworks. Brittany has also counseled patients in the hereditary cancer clinic and provided clinical services to specialty areas supporting rare disease diagnosis at Mayo Clinic. She has co-authored over 30 publications including on the topics of Lynch syndrome, rare genetic disease and molecular variant evaluation. Other experience includes supporting a large initiative to return results of exome sequencing to participants of a large biobank, building knowledge and expertise in emerging fields of genetics such as monogenic causes of kidney disease and autoinflammatory disorders and initiating a molecular variant evaluation course which teaches individuals with advanced degree backgrounds the fundamental concepts variant evaluation. Brittany is now an independent genomics consultant working with diagnostic and precision laboratories, start-ups and national advocacy programs (American Cancer Society, Scleroderma Research Foundation) to drive meaningful change in healthcare and genomic medicine. Birgit Funke, PhD, FACMG is a Board certified clinical molecular geneticist who directs clinical genetic testing for inherited conditions. She was the founding co-chair of the ClinGen cardiomyopathy VCEP as well as the co-chair of the Cardiovascular CDWG. Birgit serves as a process expert and advisor to the RYR2 VCEP; however, will not serve as a voting member.

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:42:10","data":{"uuid":"625b7a33-0031-49fc-8d79-31a0afb87544","name":"Pediatric Cataract","description":"Pediatric cataracts affect 20,000-40,000 children each year worldwide. Cataract may be an isolated finding or seen in association with one of more than 200 syndromes. Identification of the underlying genetic etiology of pediatric cataracts may clarify associated health risks, aid in prognostication, and guide medical management and family planning. Early identification of a genetic etiology can have a significant impact, such as for those with cerebrotendinous xanthomatosis, a rare lipid storage disease in which early treatment can slow or even stop disease progression. Accordingly, a missed diagnosis can delay necessary screening or treatment, potentially increasing disease morbidity and deceasing quality of life for individuals with pediatric cataracts. We hypothesize that there will be fewer missed diagnoses and improved clinical outcomes if we develop gene-specific variant classification guidelines for clinically actionable pediatric cataracts.

We therefore propose a Pediatric Cataract VCEP. We plan to begin with 4 genes associated with systemic manifestations where pediatric cataracts may be the presenting symptom of a syndrome with treatment/screening recommendations in the following order: 1. CYP27A1, 2. OCRL, 2. MAF, and 4. FTL.

Prior to transitioning work to OCRL, we will invite additional members to join the VCEP with expertise in Lowe syndrome. Additionally, MAF and FTL will undergo gene curation through the Glaucoma and Neuro-Ophthalmology GCEPs prior to our VCEP work on these genes. Both have agreed to complete this work in accordance with our anticipated Cataract","short_name":"Cataract","scope_description":"Pediatric cataracts affect 20,000-40,000 children each year worldwide. Cataract may be an isolated finding or seen in association with one of more than 200 syndromes. Identification of the underlying genetic etiology of pediatric cataracts may clarify associated health risks, aid in prognostication, and guide medical management and family planning. Early identification of a genetic etiology can have a significant impact, such as for those with cerebrotendinous xanthomatosis, a rare lipid storage disease in which early treatment can slow or even stop disease progression. Accordingly, a missed diagnosis can delay necessary screening or treatment, potentially increasing disease morbidity and deceasing quality of life for individuals with pediatric cataracts. We hypothesize that there will be fewer missed diagnoses and improved clinical outcomes if we develop gene-specific variant classification guidelines for clinically actionable pediatric cataracts.

We therefore propose a Pediatric Cataract VCEP. We plan to begin with 4 genes associated with systemic manifestations where pediatric cataracts may be the presenting symptom of a syndrome with treatment/screening recommendations in the following order:

1. CYP27A1, 2. OCRL, 2. MAF, and 4. FTL.

Prior to transitioning work to OCRL, we will invite additional members to join the VCEP with expertise in Lowe syndrome. Additionally, MAF and FTL will undergo gene curation through the Glaucoma and Neuro-Ophthalmology GCEPs prior to our VCEP work on these genes. Both conditions result in an inability to break down certain proteins and fats, leading to toxic levels of organic acids in the body. Symptoms most often present in infancy and can include vomiting, hypotonia, lethargy, and failure to thrive. Patients with these organic acidopathies can experience complications such as brain injury, coma, and death. MMA and PA both result from defective metabolism of coenzyme A-activated carboxylic acids by the mitochondria. The current members of the expert panel are experienced in clinical care, biochemical and molecular laboratory interpretation and testing, variant classification, and functional assay development."

The ClinGen PA/MMA VCEP will specify the genes causing propionic acidemia (PA) and methylmalonic acidemia (MMA). The VCEP plans to curate in a tiered approach, in order of frequency and clinical importance, variants associated with propionic acidemia and methylmalonic acidemia due to methylmalonyl-CoA mutase deficiency and genes associated with disorders included in the RUSP (recommended universal (newborn) screening panel), with the same NBS marker C3-carnitine, and in which elevated methylmalonic acid is one of the biochemical features.

Acidemia and Methylmalonic Acidemia","short_name":"PA/MMA","scope_description":"The ClinGen PA/MMA VCEP will specify the genes causing propionic acidemia (PA) and methylmalonic acidemia (MMA). The VCEP plans to curate, in order of frequency and clinical importance, the variants of eight PA - and MMA - associated genes. The Inborn Errors of Metabolism CDWG leadership decided to create this VCEP because the conditions are part of the RUSP (recommended universal (newborn) screening panel) and part of the CDWG’s mission is to curate the genes and variants on the RUSP. The CDWG leadership also decided to combine the two diseases into one VCEP because of their overlapping disease characteristics, genetic causes (e.g., autosomal recessive inheritance), and overlapping community of experts. The current members of the expert panel are experienced in clinical care, biochemical and molecular laboratory interpretation and testing, variant classification, and functional assay development.

Propionic acidemia (PA) is a metabolic condition, ranging from neonatal onset to late-onset, with neonatal onset being the most common form, and is caused by a deficiency of propionyl-coenzyme A carboxylase (PCC). PCC catalyzes the conversion of propionyl-CoA to methylmalonyl-CoA, with infants with PA showing elevated C3 (propionylcarnitine) on newborn screening. PCC is a heterodimer encoded by PCCA and PCCB. Methylmalonic acidemia (MMA) is due to the failure to convert methylmalonyl-coenzyme A (CoA) into succinyl-CoA during propionyl-COA metabolism. There can be complete or partial deficiency of the enzyme methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (MMUT) or deficiency of the enzyme methylmalonyl-CoA epimerase. In addition defects in vitamin B12 absorption and processing can lead to isolated MMA, isolated homocysteinemia, or elevations in both MMA and homocysteine. The most common of these defects is due to pathogenic variants in MMACHC. Both MMUT and MMACHC are included in the ACMG recommended expanded carrier screen.

Variant classification criteria will be adapted and specified for the eight genes, in a tiered approach. The first tier will include the \]most common methylmalonic gene (MMUT) and most common B12 deficiency gene MMACHC. The second tier will include the less common methylmalonic genes (MMAA, MMAB, MMADHC, and MCEE) and the propionic acidemia genes PCCA and PCCB. A CSpec entry and curation SOP will be created for standardization of variant classification. The ClinGen Variant Curation Interface (VCI) will be used to document evidence used for variant classification. In the case of autosomal recessive metabolic disorders, previous VCEPs have begun curation with the known pathogenic variants. This helps with the assignment of points for PM3 for rare variants with less evidence. The PA/MMA VCEP plans to use this approach as well. In addition, the VCEP intends to create a Hypothes.is annotation protocol, in the hopes of leveraging that resource for more efficient curation.

Variants in the 8 genes will be collected from the HGMD and LOVD databases, as well as ClinVar. Additional variants are expected to be identified by the VCEP members and their professional networks. As an example, ClinVar lists 1492 variants in PCCA (385 missense), 1200 variants in PCCB (380 missense), 1094 variants in MMUT (372 missense), 386 variants in MMADHC (63 missense), 586 variants in MMAA (118 misense), 558 variants in MMAB (104 missense), and 138 variants in MCEE (38 missense) (accessed: December 8, 2024).","membership_description":"The current members of the expert panel are experienced in clinical care, biochemical and molecular laboratory interpretation and testing, variant classification, and functional assay development. There are also VCEP members who are on other ClinGen VCEPs and are familiar with the ACMG/AMP guidelines to classify variants and the SOP to create specifications. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:42:08","data":{"uuid":"2bb3a361-d172-4f92-9848-f256a065e3e0","name":"Sodium and Calcium Channel Arrhythmia","description":"The Sodium and Calcium Channel Arrhythmia Variant Curation Expert Panel (VCEP) will focus on the curation and interpretation of genetic variants in the SCN5A gene. SCN5A encodes the cardiac voltage-gated sodium channel NaV1.5, which plays a critical role in the initiation and propagation of the cardiac action potential. The primary goal of this VCEP is to address the challenges of variant interpretation in SCN5A and promote accurate clinical diagnoses through rigorous adaptation and application of the ACMG/AMP classification guidelines. SCN5A has been assessed by ClinGen Gene Curation Expert Panels to have definitive gene-disease validity assertions for its associations with Brugada Syndrome (BrS), Long QT Syndrome (LQTS), and Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM). In addition, SCN5A variants can also lead to a variety of other arrhythmia conditions and symptoms. Initial discussions among VCEP members led to the conclusion that SCN5A-related arrhythmias may be best considered as a merged/spectrum phenotype, tentatively titled "SCN5A-related cardiac rhythm disorders." As part of this discussion, our group is in conversation with the HCVD GCEP to re-examine SCN5A and its curated gene-disease The Sodium and Calcium Channel Arrhythmia Variant Curation Expert Panel (VCEP) will focus on the curation and interpretation of genetic variants in the cell-surface sodium and calcium channels linked to arrhythmias. The main initial focus of the VCEP will be the *SCN5A* gene. *SCN5A* encodes the cardiac voltage-gated sodium channel NaV1.5, which plays a critical role in the initiation and propagation of the cardiac action potential. The primary goal of this VCEP is to address the challenges of variant interpretation in *SCN5A* and promote accurate clinical diagnoses through rigorous adaptation and application of the ACMG/AMP classification guidelines. *SCN5A* has been assessed by ClinGen Gene Curation Expert Panels to have definitive gene-disease validity assertions for its associations with Brugada Syndrome (BrS), Long QT Syndrome (LQTS), and Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM). In addition, *SCN5A* variants can also lead to a variety of other arrhythmia conditions and symptoms. Initial discussions among VCEP members led to the conclusion that *SCN5A*-related arrhythmias may be best considered as a merged/spectrum phenotype, tentatively titled "*SCN5A*-related cardiac rhythm disorders." As part of this discussion, our group is in conversation with the HCVD GCEP to re-examine *SCN5A* and its curated gene-disease relationship. **Example phenotypes encompassed by *SCN5A*-related cardiac rhythm disorders:** 1. **Brugada Syndrome (BrS; MONDO:0011001) and other electrophysiological phenotypes associated with *SCN5A* loss-of-function:** BrS is an inherited arrhythmia disorder characterized by ST-segment elevation in the right precordial leads on the electrocardiogram and an increased risk of severe ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death. Rare loss-of-function variants in *SCN5A* are implicated in ~20% of BrS cases, including missense, in-frame indel, nonsense, frameshift, and splice-disrupting variants. Individuals with loss-of-function *SCN5A* variants can also present with a variety of other electrophysiological phenotypes, such as bundle branch blocks, sick sinus syndrome, progressive cardiac conduction defect, and atrial standstill. Variable phenotypes are often observed for the same variant, or even within the same family. 2. **Long QT Syndrome (LQTS; MONDO:0011003)**: LQTS is characterized by a prolonged QT interval on the electrocardiogram and increased risk for *torsades de pointes* arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death. Rare gain-of-function missense and in-frame indel variants in *SCN5A* are implicated in ~5% of LQTS cases. 3. **Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM; MONDO:0011377)**: Several *SCN5A* missense variants have also been associated with DCM, a condition characterized by ventricular dilation and reduced systolic function. There is also overlap between DCM-associated *SCN5A* variants and a disorder called multiple ectopic purkinje-related premature contractions (MEPPC). However due to the rarity and complicated phenotypes of these disorders, these disease associations will not be a major initial focus of the VCEP. For *SCN5A* variants, these disorders typically have a dominant inheritance pattern, although individuals with homozygous or compound heterozygous variants can rarely occur. **Rationale for Focus on *SCN5A*** *SCN5A* is a critical gene for cardiac function and has been extensively studied in the context of arrhythmia syndromes and cardiomyopathy. It has high clinical significance, as reflected by its “definitive” ClinGen disease assertions and its presence on the ACMG Secondary Finding list of “actionable” genes. Despite its importance, the classification of *SCN5A* variants remains challenging due to: - The large number of variants, due to a large gene size (2015 aa). - A high proportion of Variants of Uncertain Significance, particularly for missense variants. - Variable expressivity and incomplete penetrance observed in *SCN5A*-related conditions. - The complexity of overlapping arrhythmia or cardiac conduction phenotypes for some variants, including additional phenotypes that have not yet been reviewed by ClinGen GCEPs. **Methodology** The *SCN5A* VCEP will first develop and refine *SCN5A*-specific variant interpretation guidelines based on the ACMG/AMP framework. Our initial guidelines will be based on the v3 ACMG criteria although we will adapt the rules to v4 criteria as these guidelines are finalized. We will then validate and refine the rules by testing them with a panel of *SCN5A* variants. Our targeted variant counts for this stage will be 20 B/LB variants, 20 P/LP variants, and 10-12 VUS. Since *SCN5A* has alternative splicing events that have potential clinical relevance, we will also recommend preferred variant nomenclature, following MANE and MANE-clinical transcript guidelines. Lastly, we will expand variant classification efforts to additional *SCN5A* variants and continue to review and revise variant classifications. **Future Directions** In the future, the group may develop and validate variant classification rules for additional sodium or calcium channel genes linked to inherited arrhythmia disorders, as asserted by relevant Clingen GCEPs. This may include *CACNA1C*, which encodes the primary subunit of the calcium channel CaV1.2.
","membership_description":"All individuals are knowledgeable of the ACMG/AMP Guidelines for Sequence Interpretation. The SCN5A VCEP has at least 4 experts with substantial experience in classifying monogenic disease variants. Daniela Macaya and Matteo Vatta hold FACMG certifications. Valeria Novelli and Jodie Ingles also have experience classifying variants and are members of other VCEPs as core approval members within ClinGen. Additionally, Jamie Vandenberg and Chai-Ann Ng are experts on other ClinGen VCEPs. The group consists of cardiologists and electrophysiologists, along with experts in cardiovascular genetics and variant interpretation. Chair Andrew Glazer, PhD is a geneticist interested in deciphering which mutations underlie rare Mendelian diseases, with a focus on arrhythmia syndromes. He has developed high-throughput functional assays for studying cardiac ion channels and used biobank and patient cohorts to study candidate disease-causing variants. He is especially interested in the challenge of deciphering the thousands of rare Variants of Unknown Significance (VUS) that exist in Mendelian disease genes, with a focus on arrhythmia-associated ion channels. Cardiologists Elijah Behr, MD, BA, MBBS, MA is an international expert in the field of arrhythmias and is Professor in Cardiovascular Medicine at St. George’s, University of London where he directs the Cardiovascular and Genomics Research Institute. He is first chair of the Predisposition and Screening Community of the Genome England Research Network and advises the NHS England Clinical Reference Groups for Cardiovascular and Genomics Services. He leads a research group with interests that include the genomics and prediction of sudden death risk; drug-induced arrhythmia; the Sudden Arrhythmic Death Syndrome (SADS) and Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS); channelopathies including long QT and Brugada syndromes; and arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy. He has co-written multiple international guideline documents and published over 300 articles in peer reviewed journals. He is a member of the Hereditary Cardiovascular Disease GCEP. Electrophysiologists Andrew Landstrom, MD, PhD is a pediatric cardiologist with expertise in pediatric electrophysiology, pediatric cardiomyopathy and heart failure, and cardiovascular genetics. He leads a basic science lab exploring the genetic and cellular causes of cardiac channelopathies, cardiomyopathies, and congenital heart disease. He is a member of the Cardiovascular CDWG, Hereditary Cardiovascular Disease GCEP, Potassium Channel Arrhythmia VCEP, and co-chair of RYR2 VCEP. Katja Odening, MD PhD is a physician-scientist from University of Bern. Her research is focused on advancing the mechanistic understanding of cardiac arrhythmogenesis in genetic arrhythmia disorders by identifying genetic, hormonal, and environmental modifiers of the individual arrhythmic risk. Gregory Webster, MD, MPH is a physician at Ann & Robert H Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago. Clinical interests include: Genetic heart rhythm disease and invasive treatment of heart rhythm disorders, including catheter ablation and heart rhythm devices. Chai-Ann Ng, PhD is a senior staff scientist at the Victor Chang Cardiac Research Institute. He leads an ion channel functional genomics group to establish well-validated functional patch clamp assays applicable to interpret variants of uncertain significance detected in patients that have cardiac and neuronal channelopathies. His research is currently supported by the MRFF - Genomics Health Futures Mission grant, and by working together with different clinical testing laboratories in Australia, the UK and the USA, the aim is to help reclassify functionally abnormal missense VUS in cardiac and neuronal channelopathies to likely pathogenic. He is also member of the Potassium Channel Arrhythmia VCEP. Jamie Vandenberg, PhD, MBBS is a group leader at the Victor Chang Cardiac Research Institute in 2002. He is a Professor in the School of Clinical Medicine at the University of New South Wales. Jamie is recognised internationally as an expert in cardiac electrophysiology, particularly for his research on hERG K+ channels and the molecular basis of inherited and drug-induced cardiac arrhythmias. He is a member of the ion channel working group advising the Food and Drug Administration on the development of better assays to screen for cardiotoxicity of all new drugs and a member of the Variant Curation Expert Panel for Cardiac K+ channels advising the ClinGen consortium on classification of variants in inherited arrhythmia syndromes. Rafik Tadros, MD PhD is a cardiologist, clinical electrophysiologist and director of the Cardiovascular Genetics Centre at the Montreal Heart Institute. He is associate professor at the Faculty of Medicine at University de Montreal, where he holds the Canada Research Chair in Translational Cardiovascular Genetics. His clinical interests are sudden death in the young and heritable heart diseases. His research program focuses on applying genomics to cardiovascular clinical care and prevention as part of personalized medicine. Clinician-Scientists Marina Cerrone, MD is a Research Associate Professor of Medicine and the Codirector of the Inherited Arrhythmias Clinic at NYU Grossmann School of Medicine. She is interested in clinical and translational studies of patients affected by Inherited Arrhythmias. Her research projects involve cellular electrophysiology, molecular biology, histology and imaging experiments in animal and cellular models, including engineered mouse models of inherited arrhythmias to study mechanisms facilitating arrhythmias and disease progression in Arrhythmogenic Cardiomyopathy. Her expertise includes functional in vivo characterization of mouse models, with ECG, echocardiography, provocative pharmacologic testing, and survival surgery. She is also a member of the Potassium Channel Arrhythmia VCEP. Dan Roden, MD’s research interests are the genetic determinants of abnormal heart rhythms, high throughput functional assessment of ion channel gene variants, and implementation of genomic and pharmacogenomic information in clinical care. After serving as chief of the division of Clinical Pharmacology for 12 years, he was tasked in 2006 with leading Vanderbilt’s efforts in Personalized Medicine. He serves as co-PI for the Vanderbilt site of the Electronic Medical Records and Genomics (eMERGE) network, the genomics Learning Healthcare System network, and the Data and Research Center for the US NIH All of Us program. Cardiovascular Genetics Benjamin Helm, PhD, MS, CGC is a Genetic counselor at Indiana University School of Medicine. Specific interests include cardiovascular genetics, congenital malformations, rare diseases, and public health genomics. Dr. Helm's work in this program includes the development of cardiovascular genetics services for outpatient and inpatient settings tailored to specific diseases. Jodie Ingles, PhD, MPH is Head of the Clinical Genomics Laboratory and Director of the Genomics and Inherited Disease Program at Garvan Institute of Medical Research. She is a cardiac genetic counsellor with more than 20 years' experience working with families with inherited heart diseases. Her research is focused on using genomics to improve diagnosis and management of cardiac patients. She is a member of the Cardiovascular CDWG, Cardiomyopathy VCEP, Dilated Cardiomyopathy GCEP, and chair of Hereditary Cardiovascular Disease GCEP. Kyla Dunn, MS, LCGC is a genetic counselor with the Pediatric Inherited Cardiovascular Disorders Program at Stanford Medicine Children’s Health in Palo Alto, California. She specializes in heritable cardiomyopathies, cardiac arrhythmia syndromes, and unexplained sudden cardiac death. Variant Interpretation Valeria Novelli, PhD is a Basic research scientist, Clinical genetic testing laboratorian from Centro Cardiologico Monzino. She also serves on the Cardiovascular CDWG, Potassium Channel Arrhythmia VCEP, RYR2 VCEP, and SVI VCEP Review Committee. Megan Roy-Puckelwartz, PhD is a research scientist and Assistant Professor at Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine. Matteo Vatta, PhD is a laboratory director at LabCorp. He also serves on several other ClinGen Panels, including the Cardiomyopathy VCEP, Congenital Heart Disease GCEP, Congenital Heart Disease VCEP, Congenital Myopathies VCEP, and Potassium Channel Arrhythmia VCEP. Roddy Walsh, PhD is a basic research scientist from St George's, University of London. He also serves on the Dilated Cardiomyopathy and Hereditary Cardiovascular Disease GCEP as an expert. Daniela Macaya, FACMG, MQC is Director of Cardiogenetics at GeneDx. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:42:06","data":{"uuid":"bc7cbed0-5416-4923-a730-8dc987a7c4ed","name":"Maculopathy","description":"The goal of this VCEP is to curate clinically relevant variants in genes associated with maculopathies. This is a distinct set of inherited retinal dystrophies where there is macular involvement damaging central vision, but peripheral vision is spared. We propose to develop ACMG/AMP rule specifications for the *PRPH2, BEST1, EFEMP1, C1QTNF5, TIMP3 *and* ELOVL4* genes. These genes are typically associated with autosomal dominant or semidominant forms of maculopathy, and those will be the prioritized phenotypes. Each gene has undergone the ClinGen lumping and splitting process, where appropriate, and has been curated for the autosomal dominant or semidominant phenotype by the ClinGen Retina GCEP and reached a definitive classification. The VCEP may consider curating autosomal recessive forms or related disease entities associated with these genes as future work. Additionally, we may consider adding additional genes associated with inherited maculopathies to this list.   We meet monthly to begin our work on the *PRPH2* gene which has previously been associated with several variants of maculopathies, including Pattern dystrophy, Stargardt disease, vitelliform macular dystrophy, central areolar choroidal dystrophy, retinitis punctata albescens and a form of Leber congenital amaurosis (LCA). The biallelic cases exhibit an earlier and more severe phenotype resembling more of the LCA diagnosis. Additionally, the molecular mechanism for all of these phenotypes appears to be the same and, therefore, represent a spectrum of semidominant disease rather than separate disease entities. Once complete, the VCEP may decide to focus on two or more genes simultaneously to complete rule specifications for other genes of The goal of this VCEP is to curate clinically relevant variants in genes associated with maculopathies. This is a distinct set of inherited retinal dystrophies where there is macular involvement damaging central vision, but peripheral vision is spared. We propose to develop ACMG/AMP rule specifications for the *PRPH2, BEST1, EFEMP1* *, C1QTNF5, TIMP3* and *ELOVL4* genes. These genes are typically associated with autosomal dominant or semidominant forms of maculopathy, and those will be the prioritized phenotypes. Each gene has undergone the ClinGen lumping and splitting process, where appropriate, and has been curated for the autosomal dominant or semidominant phenotype by the ClinGen Retina GCEP and reached a definitive classification. The VCEP may consider curating autosomal recessive forms or related disease entities associated with these genes as future work. Additionally, we may consider adding additional genes associated with inherited maculopathies to this list. We plan to meet monthly to begin our work on the *PRPH2* gene which has previously been associated with several variants of maculopathies, including Pattern dystrophy, Stargardt disease, vitelliform macular dystrophy, central areolar choroidal dystrophy, retinitis punctata albescens and a form of Leber congenital amaurosis (LCA). The biallelic cases exhibit an earlier and more severe phenotype resembling more of the LCA diagnosis. Additionally, the molecular mechanism for all of these phenotypes appears to be the same and, therefore, represent a spectrum of semidominant disease rather than separate disease entities. The Retina GCEP lumped all conditions under *PRPH2*-related retinopathy, and that is the phenotype for which this VCEP plans to curate. Once complete, the VCEP may decide to focus on two or more genes simultaneously to complete rule specifications for other genes of interest.

","membership_description":"
The Maculopathy VCEP has seven molecular geneticists, two of whom currently run clinical laboratories. Each of them are members and/or co-chairs of other ClinGen experts panels using the ACMG/AMP guidelines. This VCEP also has five genetic counselor members that qualify as having vast experience using the variant curation guidelines. One of the GC's is a former lab counselor, and the other two coordinate various ClinGen VCEPs.

The ClinGen Retina GCEP has reviewed the RHO gene and decided to consider both dominant and recessive forms of retinopathies as a spectrum of semi-dominant forms, which reaches a definitive gene/disease classification. More than 300 variants from affected individuals have been reported in the RHO gene (LOVD RHO database). Currently in ClinVar, there are nearly 600 variants reported in ClinVar with 41 conflicting classifications and over 200 variants of uncertain significance. Pathogenic variants in the RHO gene are the most common cause of non-syndromic autosomal dominant retinitis pigmentosa (adRP), accounting for 20-30% of all adRP cases, and there are nearly 100 genes associated with non-syndromic RP (PMID: 26962691, 39199291). Six different molecular mechanisms have been reported in the RHO gene, including protein misfolding, mislocalization, abnormal activation or deactivation, endocytosis, altered photoactivity and abnormal binding to the arrestin protein. Mosaicism of Vascular Anomalies","short_name":"Mosaic Vascular","scope_description":"Vascular anomalies are deviations in the structure of blood and lymphatic vessels, leading to benign, fluid-filled swellings. These conditions typically arise from sporadic somatic mutations, resulting in mosaicism. The genetic testing of individuals with vascular anomalies is vital for precise diagnosis and treatment planning. Notably, we now recognize that, in addition to many cases driven by inherited genetic alterations, these anomalies can also emerge from somatic variation occurring post-zygotically. In all instances, establishing the correct genomic diagnosis is fundamental to effective clinical management. It provides diagnostic certainty, informs prognosis, guides recurrence risk assessment, and, in some cases, opens doors to personalized treatment options.

Several genes associated with these conditions have been extensively characterized, including activating variants in the PI3K-MTOR and RAS-MAPK pathways. These genes encompass PIK3CA, AKT, MTOR, TEK, MAP2K1, BRAF, and KRAS. However, the rapid advancements in high-throughput genome sequencing over the past decade have unveiled a wealth of potentially disease-associated genes and pathogenic variants that are yet to be systematically curated into clinically actionable categories.

While gene and variant curation for germline findings has reached a relatively mature stage, somatic mosaicism has received relatively less attention. In 2023, the National Institutes of Health (NIH) initiated the Somatic Mosaicism across Human Tissues (SMaHT) Network to elucidate the impact of somatic mosaicism on human biology and health. We propose implementing a systematic ClinGen-based approach to curate findings related to somatic mosaicism in the context of vascular anomalies. This effort will facilitate the development of appropriate clinical tests and the interpretation of results. The goal is to provide precise gene-disease relationships and robust interpretations of somatic mosaic variants within the scope of vascular anomalies, advancing the field toward personalized treatments.

Accordingly, we propose establishing the Gene Curation Expert Panel (GCEP) for the Somatic Mosaicism of Vascular Anomalies to curate genes and establish gene-disease relationships related to vascular anomalies. The GCEP will be responsible for curating genes and establishing gene-disease relationships relevant to vascular anomalies. We plan to adapt the current gene-disease validity curation rules, making necessary adjustments to incorporate somatic evidence for gene curation in these conditions. Since this is the first somatic-focused GCEP, it will be important to communicate with the Gene Curation Working Group (GCWG) on a regular basis in order to assess how the current monogenic gene-disease framework is working for a somatic gene curation effort. We reached out to the GCWG and had a plan to schedule check-in meetings and provide updates to the GCWG weekly Monday meetings.

Our initial focus will be on a smaller list of genes that have characteristic somatic mutations that result in vascular anomalies and which are not just the mosaic form of a known germline syndrome. Therefore, we carefully reviewed and decided to update the gene list to focus on the following 14 genes at this time: PIK3CA, PIK3R1, RASA1, TEK, AKT1, HRAS, KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, GNA11, GNA14, GNAQ, MAP2K1, MAP3K3. While some of these genes have been curated relative to other diseases (e.g. PIK3CA- Brain malformations GCEP, Hereditary Cancer GCEP, Breast/Ovarian Cancer GCEP; BRAF- RASopathy GCEP), these genes are crucial contributors to mosaic vascular anomalies, a disease-type at risk for ascertainment bias due to variability in presentation. Thus, through the inclusion of these genes, we are thereby extending on knowledge of disease-phenotype relationships. We will utilize ClinGen Lumping and Splitting guidance during pre-curation and use the Gene Tracker to enter precuration information. For the genes listed on other expert panels, we are happy to communicate and collaborate with other groups to provide the most appropriate curation following the ClinGen guidance.","membership_description":null,"type":"gcep","date_completed":"2024-11-26T05:00:00.000000Z","inactive_date":null,"current_step":1,"all_genes":[{"hgnc_id":1097,"gene_symbol":"BRAF"},{"hgnc_id":4379,"gene_symbol":"GNA11"},{"hgnc_id":4382,"gene_symbol":"GNA14"},{"hgnc_id":4390,"gene_symbol":"GNAQ"},{"hgnc_id":5173,"gene_symbol":"HRAS"},{"hgnc_id":6407,"gene_symbol":"KRAS"},{"hgnc_id":6840,"gene_symbol":"MAP2K1"},{"hgnc_id":6855,"gene_symbol":"MAP3K3"},{"hgnc_id":7989,"gene_symbol":"NRAS"},{"hgnc_id":8975,"gene_symbol":"PIK3CA"},{"hgnc_id":8979,"gene_symbol":"PIK3R1"},{"hgnc_id":9871,"gene_symbol":"RASA1"},{"hgnc_id":11724,"gene_symbol":"TEK"},{"hgnc_id":391,"gene_symbol":"AKT1"}],"gcep_final_approval":"2024-11-26T05:00:00.000000Z"},"parent":{"uuid":"ac91a7fc-a3e0-4a67-b6ec-37fad86a8b86","name":"Other","type":"cdwg","status":"active"}}}

Oncology","short_name":"Ocular Oncology","scope_description":"RB1

The RB1 VCEP will first focus on the tumor suppressor gene RB1, which has a ClinGen Gene-Disease Validity assertion of definitive for the phenotype retinoblastoma (MONDO:0008380). As there is an anticipated updated ACMG classification document pending, we plan to initiate our work, using the new protocol/document as part of the basis of our RB1 VCEP specifications.

The retinoblastoma gene (RB1) was the first tumor suppressor gene identified based on location in the region of 13q14 germline deletion in some patients with retinoblastoma. Retinoblastoma is a malignant tumor of the developing retina that occurs in children, usually before age five years. Retinoblastoma may be unifocal or multifocal. Since then, germline pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants in RB1 have been associated with Pineoblastoma, as well as adolescent and adult-onset cancers such as sarcomas and melanomas. Of note, the pRB protein interacts with more than 200 partners and the pathway is inactivated in more than half of all cancers.

The disease-causing variant spectrum includes missense, nonsense, frameshift, in-frame, splice-site, deep intronic, 5’- and 3’ -UTR variations, with null variants associated with the highest penetrance. RB1 germline variants are often familial but have a de novo rate of up to 80%.

RB1 is included on most multi-gene cancer panels, often sent in adults for other cancer indications, as well as whole exome/genome sequencing as an ACMG secondary finding, introducing a complexity in interpretation given the early pediatric onset of retinoblastoma. Therefore, variant interpretation will need to assess epidemiology and single pedigrees as well as functional assays in order to achieve accurate clinical diagnosis, which will determine whether variant harboring individuals are recommended any clinical and imaging surveillance.

BAP1

Following completion of variant classification guidelines for the RB1 gene, the VCEP will focus on curation for the BAP1 gene. The BAP1 gene has a ClinGen Gene-Disease Validity assertion for BAP1-related predisposition syndrome (MONDO:0013692). This may require inviting additional experts in BAP1 and related tumor types to the group.

The BAP1 gene, a tumor suppressor gene, was first described in 2011. It encodes the BRCA1-associated protein, a nuclear-localized deubiquitinase involved in several cellular processes including repair of DNA double-strand breaks through homologous recombination and apoptosis.

Pathogenic/likely pathogenic germline variants in BAP1 are associated with uveal (eye) melanoma (UM), malignant mesothelioma (MMe), cutaneous melanoma (CM), renal cell carcinoma (RCC), and basal cell carcinoma (BCC). Hepatocellular carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, and meningioma may also be associated with BAP1 germline variants. Germline variants in BAP1 can also be associated with other cutaneous findings such as BAP1-inactivated melanocytic tumor (formerly called atypical Spitz tumor) and onychopapilloma.

The disease-causing variant spectrum in the literature and ClinVar is mostly comprised of protein-truncating (nonsense, frameshift, splice-site, etc) variants, although other variant types such as missense variants have been reportedly more rarely. Notably, de novo missense variants in the UCH domain and nuclear-localization signal domain have recently been reported in individuals with a neurodevelopmental disorder without clear tumor predisposition called Kury-Isidor syndrome . The VCEP will not be curating variants associated with Kury-Isidor syndrome.

Approximately 88% of probands and 82.5% of relatives with a germline likely pathogenic/pathogenic BAP1 germline variant having had a cancer diagnosis. However, the true penetrance is unclear due to potential ascertainment bias in initial studies. The proportion of individuals with BAP1-related tumor predisposition with a de novo variant is unknown, as most cases described in the literature are familial.

BAP1 germline variants have multiple layers of complexity in variant interpretation including reduced penetrance variable expressivity, and limited information regarding missense variants. A concerted effort is needed to reduce the number of variants of unknown significance, which represent ~44% of all variants in ClinVar to date. This will be especially important as BAP1 is increasingly included on multi-gene cancer panels.","membership_description":"The suggested expert panel will be directed by three Co-PIs, Dr. Arupa Ganguly from the USA, Dr. Hilary Racher from Canada and Dr. Dietmar Lohmann from Germany. The full Expert Panel consists of an additional 21 experts in basic sciences, and diagnosis and management of retinoblastoma, 7 of whom have prior experience with ClinGen VCEPs. The group includes members across 6 countries, and within the United States includes representation from both coasts and the central US. The VCEP also has pending invitations to additional experts from Vietnam, Nigeria, and Ethiopia.

Panel members are providing expertise in (ACMG-based) application of variant calling and sign-out through experience in academic and commercial ‘high-throughput’ laboratories, such as Elizabeth Chao, and co-chairs Arupa Ganguly and Hilary Racher as well as basic research of retinoblastoma and other ocular cancers. In addition, there is clinical research and clinical care expertise. VCEP leadership will continue working to identify biocurators as needed to sustain variant curation.

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:42:02","data":{"uuid":"c21de5f9-896e-4204-80c7-65f35ff964b2","name":"Childhood, Adolescent and Young Adult Cancer Predisposition","description":"Technological advances have led to increased understanding of the cancer predisposition disorders (CPDs) that result in an increased risk of childhood cancer. However, compared to adult cancers, establishing the clinical validity of genes for these disorders can prove challenging due to fewer studies, smaller sample sizes and a limited number of resources/databases focused on cancer risk in children and young adults. The Childhood, Adolescent and Young Adult Cancer Predisposition GCEP (CAYA GCEP) aims to curate and evaluate the gene-disease relationships (GDRs) primarily associated with these CPDs. The CAYA GCEP will review and initially curate 120 genes identified from OMIM, literature review and the hereditary gene panels used to assess cancer susceptibility by seven large pediatric institutions. While chosen for their association with CAYA, the GCEP will curate for all oncologic manifestations associated with a gene, regardless of age of onset. Among the identified, currently uncurated genes there is an enrichment of genes associated with predisposition to haematologic malignancies (HM) and so the CAYA GCEP will take a tiered approach starting first with 68 HM genes followed by 52 genes associated with CNS/solid tumours. The CAYA GCEP will include members from the Myeloid Malignancy VCEP, plans to work closely with the Hereditary Cancer GCEP and will collaborate with other GCEPs where there is an overlap. Thirteen genes from the gene list have a published ClinGen curation. For the genes where lumping and splitting shows a separate cancer predisposition assertion that has not been curated, these will either be treated as a split curation or a new GDR by our GCEP (depending on the evidence). We will also reach out to the relevant GCEPs to provide secondary contributions to curated syndromes.\n\nThe work of the CAYA GCEP will provide a critical foundation for the gene-disease associations in the CAYA oncology population, facilitating further refinement of hereditary cancer testing panels in clinical and cancer result in an increased risk of childhood cancer. However, compared to adult cancers, establishing the clinical validity of genes for these disorders can prove challenging due to fewer studies, smaller sample sizes and a limited number of resources/databases focused on cancer risk in children and young adults. The Childhood, Adolescent and Young Adult Cancer Predisposition GCEP (CAYA GCEP) aims to curate and evaluate the gene-disease relationships (GDRs) primarily associated with these CPDs. The CAYA GCEP will review and initially curate 120 genes identified from OMIM, literature review and the hereditary gene panels used to assess cancer susceptibility by seven large pediatric institutions. While chosen for their association with CAYA, the GCEP will curate for all oncologic manifestations associated with a gene, regardless of age of onset. Among the identified, currently uncurated genes there is an enrichment of genes associated with predisposition to haematologic malignancies (HM) and so the CAYA GCEP will take a tiered approach starting first with 68 HM genes followed by 52 genes associated with CNS/solid tumours. The CAYA GCEP will include members from the Myeloid Malignancy VCEP, plans to work closely with the Hereditary Cancer GCEP and will collaborate with other GCEPs where there is an overlap. Thirteen genes from the gene list have a published ClinGen curation. For the genes where lumping and splitting shows a separate cancer predisposition assertion that has not been curated, these will either be treated as a split curation or a new GDR by our GCEP (depending on the evidence). We will also reach out to the relevant GCEPs to provide secondary contributions to curated syndromes.\n\nThe work of the CAYA GCEP will provide a critical foundation for the gene-disease associations in the CAYA oncology population, facilitating further refinement of hereditary cancer testing panels in clinical and cancer Neurovascular GCEP will use the ClinGen Gene-Disease Clinical Validity framework to assess the gene:disease relationship for genes involved in neurovascular disorders, including (but not limited to) stroke and related etiologies. The experts in the Neurovascular GCEP are extensively knowledgeable in both the clinical and research realm of neurovascular disease including stroke (ischemic and hemorrhagic), CADASIL, intracranial arteriopathies and imaging biomarkers of cerebrovascular disease. The group is composed of geneticists and basic scientists with experience in mechanistic studies of cerebrovascular disease, and translational and clinical researchers focused mostly on cerebrovascular disease. We currently have 3 biocurators on the panel, and have advertised for additional biocurator volunteers in the hopes of gaining more curators by the time the application is approved.

This group will consult the ClinGen Gene Curation WG and the Curated Disease Entity WG if questions regarding lumping and splitting arise while curating neurovascular-related genes. We will actively communicate with coordinators from the GCEPs on which we have overlapping gene lists in order to pursue collaborations and ensure that no work is duplicated.

The proposed gene list has been compiled with active input by the members of the Neurovascular GCEP. We identified a list of phenotypes that would be prioritized in this working group, and generated a list of genes that relate to these phenotypes and that are presumed to be causal (NB: the decisions were influenced by this publication: PMID: 36253534). Additionally, the gene list was cross-referenced with the two Neurovascular Disorders panels currently offered by Invitae. The GCEP recognizes that the genetics of neurovascular disease is a quickly and rapidly expanding field, which means additional genes may need to be added in the future.

All genes on the list generated by the GCEP’s experts were searched on the ClinGen website to determine if they had been previously curated, and for which disease entity/entities. The current gene list includes 46 genes that the Neurovascular GCEP plans to curate, with some being involved in more than one disorder. The proposed gene list prioritized the genes that have not yet been curated (first priority), followed by the genes that have been previously curated for one phenotype but need to be curated for a neurovascular phenotype (second priority). We recognize that the list of second priority genes is long, but the genes are included because the experts determined that they are applicable to neurovascular Ataxia","short_name":"Ataxia","scope_description":"The focus of the ClinGen Ataxia Gene Curation Expert Panel (GCEP) is monogenic disorders causing phenotypes that include cerebellar ataxia, either in isolation or as a predominant feature of a complex syndrome. To prevent confusion regarding this point, it is important to clarify that the Ataxia GCEP will not be curating genes solely on the symptom of ataxia but on the full spectrum of disease associated with each gene to be curated. The Ataxia GCEP will utilize the ClinGen Gene-Disease Clinical Validity framework to assess clinical validity for genes associated with phenotypes that include cerebellar ataxia. This may include genes with ataxia as the principal diagnostic clinical finding as well as those with ataxia as a common major secondary clinical finding in the setting of a more complex syndrome. The Ataxia GCEP is comprised of experts with extensive knowledge in the clinical, molecular, and/or genetic aspects of these various forms of cerebellar ataxias and their related genes. To select this panel we solicited 31 individuals (42% women) representing 26 academic institutions across 8 countries (35% international). Eight (8) individuals (26%) declined the invitation, 5 did not respond to the invitation (16%), and 2 were removed due to involvement with other ClinGen efforts (7%). The final expert panel consisted of 16 individuals (50% women) representing 13 academic institutions across 3 countries (19% international). One member withdrew due to time commitments after meetings had begun leaving 15 members (47% women, 12 institutions, 13% international). Currently the Ataxia GCEP has 3 biocurators and we are in the process of recruiting additional numbers, with a goal of at least 5-7 active biocurators. At this time none of the expert panel members have been designated as biocurators but the option remains available should any choose to do so.

Ataxia Gene Curation List Development Strategy: It is important to note that there are several hundred genes (~600+) associated with symptoms of ataxia, either in isolation or as part of a complex disease syndrome, including those represented on gene panels from commercial diagnostic laboratories. To examine all such genes would be challenging so the Ataxia GCEP has chosen to focus on those genes that are either most frequently observed as causative of disease in ataxia patient populations or those disorders that most commonly present with cerebellar ataxia as a predominant clinical symptom. A proposed gene list of 97 genes was compiled and agreed upon by the members of the Ataxia GCEP and organized into two tiers based on priority. The list was consistent with major review articles on the subject of cerebellar ataxia genetics (e.g., PMIDs 17303531 , 20723845 , 15099544, 29891078, 29325611, 29891056, and 29325610) and relevant online resources including the Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man (OMIM; https://www.omim.org) database. Twenty-one (21) of these genes have not previously been curated by ClinGen GCEPs and were therefore selected to represent the Ataxia GCEP Gene Curation List (Table 1). Additional genes associated with ataxia disorders may be added to the curation list in the future as warranted. We recognize that some genes on the list may be involved in more than one clinical disorder. This panel will consult with the ClinGen Gene Curation Working Group and the Lumping and Splitting Working Group as needed if questions or conflicts arise while curating these ataxia-related genes.

Prioritization of Ataxia Genes and Rationale: The Ataxia GCEP has designated certain genes on the Ataxia GCEP Gene Curation List as Tier 1, meaning those genes that are well established clinically and in the scientific community as causative of cerebellar ataxia disorders. These Tier 1 genes will consequently be curated initially. Following completion of curation for the Tier 1 genes, the Ataxia GCEP will address the genes categorized as Tier 2 in Table 1.

Brief Review of Prior Curations of Genes Associated with Ataxia Disorders: 76 of the original 97 genes designed by the Ataxia GCEP as ataxia-associated were found to have gene-disease clinical validity assessments already performed or pending curation by other GCEPs (Table 2). For those genes, the Ataxia GCEP proposes to briefly review the published curations to confirm the inclusion of the relevant ataxia-associated conditions (e.g., disorders that include cerebellar ataxia either in isolation or as part of a more complex disease presentation). If the Ataxia GCEP feels the published curation requires updating to include such disorders due to advancements in clinical knowledge in the interim since the original curation, we will reach out to the publishing GCEP regarding this. No duplicate curations of published or pending genes will be performed by the Ataxia Albinism is a congenital genetic condition characterized by variably decreased pigmentation of tissues in the eye and skin. Affected persons typically have visual impairment principally stemming from differences in eye development. Resulting final vision varies among affected individuals, ranging from mild to profound low vision. The Albinism VCEP will initially generate criteria for the TYR (OMIM 606933) and OCA2 (OMIM 611409) genes, for which ClinGen GCEP association work is complete. They are the two most common isolated albinism associated genes. The group will submit remaining likely-associated albinism genes for Glaucoma/Neuro GCEP review, and curate them if work formally documenting a strong association is completed. These genes fall under the current scope for the VCEP which may be expanded later to include more genes.
