Tumor Predisposition","short_name":"ENDO-TPS","scope_description":"Overview The ENDO-TPS VCEP is composed of 3 main subcommittees, 1) SDHx-related paraganglioma syndrome (SDHAF2, SDHA, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD) and TMEM127/MAX – related hereditary pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma syndrome, 2) Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 2 syndrome (RET) and 3) Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1) and related syndromes (CDKN1B, CDC73). All proposed genes have ClinGen Gene-Disease Validity curations for their respective endocrine indication (hereditary pheochromocytoma-paraganglioma (MONDO:0017366); multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2A (MONDO:0008234); multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2B (MONDO:0008082); multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MONDO:0007540); multiple endocrine neoplasia type 4 (MONDO:0012552); hyperparathyroidism 2 with jaw tumors (MONDO:0007768)) and have been assigned “definitive” classifications. As there is an anticipated updated ACMG classification document pending, we plan to initiate our work, using the new protocol/document as part of the basis of our ENDO-TPS VCEP specifications. Subcommittee 1 (PGL) will cover a total of 7 genes due to the broad overlap of the phenotypes associated with pathogenic variants in those genes; and the gene-specific ACMG/AMP classification criteria will be established sequentially. The plan is to start with variant analysis and construction of a gene-specific variant calling system for SDHB, which is a relatively prevalent genetic cause for HPGL-syndromes with sufficient data available. Following the developed structure for SDHB, we will next analyse other SDHx, TMEM127 and MAX variants. Once all expert panel members are familiar with the process, subcommittees 2 (MEN2) & 3 (MEN1+) will be initiated. Endocrine Tumor Predisposition Syndromes (ETPS) are amongst the clinically best understood hereditary tumor syndromes. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1) and Type 2 (MEN2) have initially been described >50 years ago. About two decades ago the SDHx-related hereditary paraganglioma syndromes (SDHx-HPGLs) were described and have become the most prevalent ETPS. In addition, several MEN1-like ETPS have been described. Variant interpretation and classification for these disease-causative genes is challenging due to the broad mutational spectrum detected in the five genes arising from missense, nonsense, frameshift, in-frame, splice-site, deep intronic, 5’- and 3`-UTR variation. In general, all ETPS-causing genes follow the principle of loss-of-function with the exception of RET variants causing MEN2, which largely occur in hot-spots and represent activating mutations of the RET kinase. The unique feature of paternal-only risk inheritance exists for SDHD, and possibly SDHAF2 and MAX. Reoccurring variants in the ETPS-causing genes are explained by either activating variants (RET) or founder variants (e.g. SDHD); de novo mutations are rare with the exception of MEN2B caused by p.M918T, which occurs de novo in up to 50%. Additionally, the degree of penetrance associated with different genes and variants is a particular challenge, ranging from 80% tumor risk (SDHD p.P81L) to low tumor risk (e.g. RET p.V804M, SDHA p.R31X \\[and other SDHA variants in general\\]). Therefore, variant interpretation will need to assess epidemiology and single pedigrees as well as functional assays in order to achieve accurate clinical diagnosis, which will determine whether variant harboring individuals are recommended any clinical and imaging surveillance, or even prophylactic thyroidectomy, as in the case of a subset of MEN2 patients.\n\nSDHx-related hereditary paraganglioma syndrome (SDHAF2, SDHA, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD) and TMEM127/MAX – related hereditary pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma syndrome subcommittee - PGL-subcommittee SDHx-HPGLs are caused by pathogenic variants in SDHAF2, SDHA, SDHB, SDHC, or SDHD. The syndrome is characterized by paragangliomas (and adrenal paragangliomas = pheochromocytomas), which arise from paraganglial cells which are associated with autonomous paraganglia. There are significant gene-dependent differences in preferences of paraganglioma (PGL) localizations. For example SDHD- and SDHC-related PGL commonly arise in the head and neck area, while SDHB-related PGL are also commonly observed in the abdomen. There is also a significant difference in life-time risk associated with variants in the different genes, e.g. SDHD (up to 80%) and SDHA (<5%). In addition there is a risk increase for renal cell carcinoma, which for SDHB variant carriers is about 5%. While there is some gene-phenotype (PGL) and gene-life-time-risk association, there is less support for variant specific differences. SDHx genes encode proteins of the mitochondrial citrate cycle succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) complex. Absence of a functional SDH-complex leads to an increase in citrate cycle metabolites and the exclusive utilization of aerobic glycolysis. As a sequelae of increased succinate and α-ketoglutarate nuclear DNA becomes hypermethylated and cells express a ‘pseudo-hypoxic’ state, which is believed to be a crucial part of tumorigenesis due to SDHx variants. In addition, absent SDH-subunits prohibit the correct assembly of the SDH complex and SDHB staining is usually absent on immunohistochemistry. This observation, alongside other evidence including co-segregation, calibrated in silico predictions and functional assays are combined in ACMG-AMP classification model. TMEM127 and MAX have mainly been reported to increase the risk for pheochromocytomas, but PGL can occur. TMEM127 encodes a transmembrane protein which signals through the mTOR pathway; germline variants are associated with a ~30% lifetime risk of pheochromocytomas, and less commonly paragangliomas (9%) or renal cell cancers (5%). MAX encodes a nuclear transcription factor which heterodimerizes with MYC or MXD1 to regulate gene transcription; germline variants are associated with a relatively high penetrance of pheochromocytoma (often bilateral) and sometimes paragangliomas. MAX variants have also been associated with a range of other endocrine and non-endocrine neoplasias including ganglioneuroma, ganglioneuroblastoma, adrenomedullary hyperplasia, pituitary neuroendocrine tumors, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, parathyroid adenomas and renal oncocytomas giving rise to its description as Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 5 (MEN5).\n\nMultiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 2 syndrome (RET) subcommittee - MEN2/RET subcommittee Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2 (MEN2) predisposes to medullary thyroid cancer, pheochromocytoma and primary hyperparathyroidism. The clinical expression of the disease is highly variable but a strong genotype-phenotype risk correlation has been detected for some variants, e.g. ranging from life time risk for MTC of about 4%with the p.V804M variant to invariable onset of MTC during the first year of life with the MEN2B-causing p.M918T variant. MEN2 is caused by PVs in the RET proto-oncogene. RET is a membrane-bound tyrosine kinase that interacts with different membrane receptors. Point mutations, representing PV, occur in either the extracellular cysteine-rich domains, resulting in receptor activation independent dimerization and activation of signalling, or the intracellular tyrosine-rich domain, leading to ligand independent signalling. In contrast to most other hereditary cancer syndromes, which are caused by PV in tumor suppressor genes, RET PVs are mostly found in distinct hotspots that confer different degrees of constitutive signalling activity of the encoded tyrosine kinase. Several studies have focused on the effects of RET variants on intracellular signalling activity. The MEN2A/RET subcommittee will therefore be able to consider clinical, epidemiological considerations and cellular, functional systems in characterization of variants and construction of a RET specific variant calling system.\n\nMultiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1) and related syndromes (CDKN1B, CDC73) subcommittee - MEN1+ committee MEN1 is caused by PV in MEN1 (aka MENIN). This condition is clinically very well defined with an increase in risk for primary hyperparathyroidism, which is observed in the majority of patients by the 5th decade of life, neuroendocrine tumors of the foregut, particularly duodenum, pancreas, stomach and lung, and pituitary tumors, most commonly prolactinomas, but also other functional or non-functional pituitary adenomas. There had been the long notion that there are several MEN1-like families, in which no MEN1 PVs were found. A proportion of these are caused by PV in CDKN1B and the condition has also been termed Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 4. There is also a significant overlap to other syndromes, specifically hereditary predisposition to pHPT, such as the hyperparathyroidism-jaw tumor syndrome, caused by CDC73 PV. CDKN1B acts as a cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor. The molecular functions of MEN1 and CDC73 are not completely understood. MEN1 encodes a 610bp protein with nuclear localization. MEN1 is a structurally fairly unique protein, lacking larger domain homologies to other proteins. It has been shown to be involved in multiple cellular processes, such as cell cycle progression, cell division, cell motility, cell signaling, cytoskeletal structure, DNA repair, genomic stability, and transcriptional regulation. However, there are limited functional assays available. CDC73 is a component of the PAF1 complex, regulating RNA polymerase 2 related transcription. There are also studies suggestive of CDC73 as part of this complex integrates some signalling pathways, such as ATR signalling. As for MEN1, molecular assays on protein function are very limited. The Expert Panel of the MEN1+ committee will begin after some experience has been gained with the PGL-subcommittee and initially focus on MEN1 variants, and later address CDKN1B and CDC73 variants.","membership_description":"The suggested expert panel will be directed by two Co-Pis, Dr. Roderick Clifton-Bligh from Australia and Dr. Tobias Else from the USA. The full Expert Panel consists of an additional 26 experts in basic sciences, and syndrome diagnosis and management of Hereditary Endocrine Tumor Syndromes, including hereditary predisposition to Pheochromocytoma and Paraganglioma (Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2 \\[RET\\], Hereditary pheochromocytoma/Paraganglioma Syndrome \\[MAX, TMEM127, SDHx\\]) and Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1) and related Syndromes (Hyperparathyroidism and Jaw Tumor Syndrome \\[CDC73\\], Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 4 \\[CDKN1B\\]). Panel members are providing expertise in (ACMG-based) application of variant calling (9), basic research of endocrine tumor syndromes (10) with a focus on pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma as well as Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1-associated syndromes. In addition, there is clinical research and clinical care expertise regarding these predisposition syndromes. Academic and commercial ‘high-throughput’ laboratories are represented in the panel. Several members have participated in other VCEP enterprises. VCEP leadership will continue working to identify biocurators and a biocurator trainer as the expert panel begins discussion of specifications for the initial gene, SDHB. The panel of experts includes endocrinologists, clinical and molecular geneticists, scientists and clinicians with familiarity of variant calling, genetic counselors, pathologists and epidemiologists. The clinicians in the panel participate in the diagnosis and/or clinical management of Endocrine Tumor Syndromes. Several participants have collaborated with each other on aspects of research pertinent to Endocrine Tumor Syndromes through the European Network for the Study of Adrenal Tumors (ENSAT) and the {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:57","data":{"uuid":"6417d0e1-6cdf-4b03-be6c-5e371c61dae4","name":"Established Significance SC-","description":"The Variant of Established Clinical Significance (VECS) SC-VCEP’s mission is to curate and maintain a database of  
clinical interpretations for all somatic variants of established clinical significance. CIViC is a publicly curated  
knowledgebase that is populated by the cancer research and clinical communities based on their individual priorities  
and expertise. Therefore, it is expected that there will be significant gaps in representation of even the most clinically  
relevant variants. Furthermore, as ClinGen Somatic seeks to establish an FDA-recognized genetic variant database  
within CIViC, including such variants will be important for this process, as well as ensuring that CIViC continues to  
be comprehensive and valuable for the oncology community. We expect that all curated variants will be classified as  
AMP/ASCO/CAP tier/level IA and most will also be classified as oncogenic or likely oncogenic (where applicable) by  
the ClinGen/CGC/VICC criteria. Our initial scope will focus on variants associated with FDA cleared or approved  
companion diagnostics. The proposed genes are limited to BRAF, EGFR, ERBB2, ESR1, EZH2, FGFR3, FLT3,  
IDH1, IDH2, KIT, KRAS, MET, NRAS, PIK3CA, and {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:57","data":{"uuid":"faa17ff8-7987-415d-a1e0-eaa8c43674a0","name":"EGFR Somatic Cancer","description":"The Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR) is a tyrosine kinase receptor, which activates pathways involved in control of cell proliferation and growth (PMID: 24312144). Various molecular alterations can affect EGFR in cancers, including missense mutations, fusions and copy number changes. Missense variants in EGFR play a critical role in many common cancers like lung cancer, where they dictate up front therapy choice, but due to the complexity of EGFR fusions, and a field-wide need for expert curation of this class of variants, they have been chosen to be our initial primary focus. EGFR fusions occur with a variety of partner genes, and are common in various cancer types. They also serve as diagnostic/prognostic/therapeutic markers (PMID: 19922469, 21670455, 26843189, 26416732). EGFR fusion can be a primary event, but also occur as the result of more complex molecular alterations, such as amplification of chr7p11.2 segment with concurrent fusions, where the latter might be a “passenger” event. To address the classification challenges of EGFR fusions, the SC-VCEP will begin with pre-curation data collection to create a comprehensive list of EGFR fusions put together from public fusion databases (Mitelman database, Fusion GDB, TCGA, COSMIC), as well as from clinical laboratory patient lists and the published literature. The group will work with the CVI and ClinGen Somatic Cancer Clinical Domain Working Group on fusion guidance to avoid duplicating effort done by other SC-VCEPs. While fusion oncogenicity guidance has been developed by the NTRK fusion SC-VCEP (PMID: 35366592), EGFR is a unique receptor with ligand-dependent and ligand-independent mechanisms of activation, which can involve the extracellular domain (for example in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma), or intracellular domains including TKD (NSCLC). All of those mechanisms are outside of the kinase domain, distinct from NTRK fusions, where the fusion activation mechanism is kinase domain dependent. We will therefore develop new curation rules for novel mechanisms specific for EGFR fusions in collaboration with the CVI. Additionally, the SC-VCEP will curate from literature the therapeutic response of EGFR fusions to different generations of FDA approved targeted therapies, using AMP/ASCO/CAP guidelines (PMID: 27993330) to generate predictive Assertions in CIViC based on evidence from published literature. Following review and approval by the CVI, our specifications will be piloted and adjusted accordingly. Application of these specifications will be used to create an extensive public resource of curated EGFR fusions in the CIViC knowledgebase. Subsequent to the development and establishment of EGFR fusion classification guidance, our SC-VCEP will turn its attention to the evaluation of other EGFR alterations, including most recurrent SNVs, EGFR amplification, and intragenic deletion ACMG/AMP variant classification framework for the PKP2 and DSP genes through disease- and/or gene-informed specifications and strength adjustments of certain rules. PKP2 and DSP were selected as priority genes because (1) both have been shown to be definitive evidence genes for ARVC by the ClinGen ARVC GCEP, (2) heterozygous PKP2 variants are the most commonly identified cause of classical ARVC, detected in up to 60% of genotype-positive index cases in different cohorts and up to 90% of familial cases of ARVC; while DSP variants cause a distinctive form of arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy that tends to disproportionately involve the left ventricle and is associated with considerable morbidity.","membership_description":"The work of Desmosomal Cardiomyopathy VCEP will build on a long-standing collaboration between the Johns Hopkins University, University Hospitals in Utrecht, Amsterdam and Groningen, the University College London, and the University of Padova. Besides other multicenter studies, our previous experience includes collaboration at the ClinGen ARVC Gene Curation Expert Panel (James et al. Circ Genom Precis Med. 2021;14:e003273. DOI: 10.1161/CIRCGEN.120.003273). For the present VCEP, we have again assembled a panel of clinical, academic, and laboratory experts in the fields of cardiovascular genetics and precision medicine, particularly specialized in ARVC and other cardiomyopathies.

Cynthia A. James, PhD, CGC is genetic counselor and the Research Director of the Johns Hopkins Center for Inherited Heart Diseases. Her research focuses on cardiovascular genetic counseling and arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathies. Her team is currently engaged in investigating the interplay of genotype and lifestyle on penetrance and course of ARVC as well as improving early detection and risk stratification in ARVC. Further research includes psychosocial adaptation to inherited heart disease and informing genetics / genomics policy through studying the experiences of families. Her research over the past 20 years has been critical in shaped the current clinical practice guidelines for ARVC. She regularly uses the ACMG/AMP guidelines since its publication in 2015, most recently in a research context. She was Co-Chair of the ClinGen ARVC Gene Curation Expert Panel and lead author of the resulting publication (James et al. Circ Genom Precis Med. 2021;14:e003273. DOI: 10.1161/CIRCGEN.120.003273).

J. Peter van Tintelen, MD, PhD is clinical geneticist and Chair of the Department of Genetics at the University Medical Center Utrecht, the Netherlands, a high-volume reference center for clinical genetic testing. His clinical experience includes supervision of genetic test interpretation and reporting. In addition to patient care, and teaching, he is involved in research and innovation. His research focuses on: a/identifying genetic factors underlying and contributing to rare disease with a focus on cardiac disease, b/understanding factors contributing to clinical variability and c/ understanding barriers that lead to the sub-optimal uptake of clinical and genetic screening for inherited disease. He has over 20 years of experience in ARVC and other cardiomyopathies, and regularly uses the ACMG/AMP guidelines since its publication in 2015. He served as Co-Chair of the ClinGen ARVC Gene Curation Expert Panel.

Brittney Murray, MS, CGC is the Lead Genetic Counselor at Johns Hopkins Center for Inherited Heart Diseases, with over 13 years of experience in the field. She regularly uses the ACMG/AMP guidelines on a daily basis, especially for ARVC, since its publication in 2015. She has been a member of ClinGen Gene Curation Expert Panels for Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy and Dilated Cardiomyopathy.

Ronald Lekanne Deprez, PhD is an experienced Laboratory Specialist Clinical Genetics certified by the Board for Clinical Laboratory Geneticists, a Professional Branch of the European Board of Medical Genetics as well as by the Clinical Genetic Laboratory Diagnostics Association (VKGL, the Netherlands), which authorizes him to sign off laboratory reports in the Netherlands. He regularly uses the ACMG/AMP guidelines to classify variants since 2015 and reviews variants during clinical laboratory case sign-out. He is also authorized by the VKGL to supervise professional training in cyto- and molecular genetics at the Amsterdam University Medical Center. He has been a member of ClinGen Gene Curation Expert Panels for ARVC and DCM.

Dennis Dooijes, PhD is an experienced Laboratory Specialist Clinical Genetics certified by the Clinical Genetic Laboratory Diagnostics Association (VKGL, the Netherlands), which authorizes him to sign off laboratory reports in the Netherlands. He regularly uses the ACMG/AMP guidelines to classify variants since 2015 and reviews variants during clinical laboratory case sign-out.

Jan D.H. Jongbloed, PhD is an experienced Laboratory Specialist Clinical Genetics certified by the Clinical Genetic Laboratory Diagnostics Association (VKGL, the Netherlands), which authorizes him to sign off laboratory reports in the Netherlands. He regularly uses the ACMG/AMP guidelines to classify variants since 2015 and reviews variants during clinical laboratory case sign-out. He has been a member of ClinGen Gene Curation Expert Panel for ARVC.

Kalliopi Pilichou, PhD is a molecular biologist with specialization in medical genetics. Her lab performs sequencing of ~1500 patients’ exomes and ~800 cascade family screenings per year. Dr. Pilichou is responsible for genetic variant interpretation using the ACMG/AMP guidelines and clinical counseling of patients. Her main research field is genetics of inherited cardiomyopathies with particular attention on the clinical application of molecular and pathological mechanisms underlying the pathogenesis. With her studies she linked for the first time desmoglein-2 gene to ARVC and then she engineered an experimental mouse model reproducing the same genetic defect, to study the disease onset and mechanism by electrophysiology, histology, ultramicroscopy, and molecular biology. She has been a member of ClinGen Gene Curation Expert Panels for ARVC and DCM.

Petros Syrris, PhD is an experienced basic research scientist with decades-long expertise in the molecular mechanisms of pathogenesis and genotype-phenotype associations in ARVC. He has been involved in the interpretation of genetic variants related to cardiac desmosomal disease through genetic and clinical research and has been a member of ClinGen Gene Curation Expert Panels for ARVC and DCM.

Melissa Kelly, MS, CGC is an experienced genetic counselor and ClinGen contributor who has worked as the coordinator for the development of MYH7-specific adaptation of the ACMG/AMP rules and first author of the VCEP report (Kelly et al. Disease","short_name":"CMT","scope_description":"Revision #2 Dear ClinGen CDWG OC Chairs: We are responding to the committee's request for more information on SORD:

"The reviewers have requested you revise your application to address the following concerns: SORD needs a completed gene curation before starting on variant classification rule specifications"

SORD gene has been curated and published on the ClinGen site. Please refer to the link below.

https://search.clinicalgenome.org/kb/genes/HGNC:11184

Revision #1 (Completed) Dear ClinGen CDWG OC Chairs:

We have prepared a response to the CMT VCEP application Step 1 submission.

We were asked to provide the following: The reviewers have requested you revise your application to address the following concerns: • We would like to see a greater number of individuals with ACMG/AMP variant classification expertise. The VCEP process is highly driven by adaptation of the rules and while general expertise in the clinical field is valuable, most VCEPs have at least 3 if not more members with regular clinical variant sign-out duties as part of their role. The CDWG OC Chairs would also like you to know: • Please articulate a plan for getting gene curations approved (which could be asking the General GCEP to do these) and also define a plan for reconciling what disease entities you will curate on. • Please provide a more formal scope statement with the rationale for these specific CMT genes given the large number of genes underlying this disorder in addition to the question about enough individuals with ACMG/AMP classification experience.

CMT VCEP Application Step 1: Response

Response #1: Individuals with ACMG/AMP variant classification expertise.

Addition of a third person: Dr Sat Dev Batish (Athena diagnostics)

Sat Dev Batish, Ph.D., FACMGG, FAAN Senior Director, Genetics Diplomate of American Board of Medical Genetics Clinical Molecular Genetics and Clinical Cytogenetics

Currently the 3 individuals with ACMG/AMP variant classification expertise are:

1)Sat Dev Batish, Ph.D., FACMGG, FAAN Senior Director, Genetics Diplomate of American Board of Medical Genetics Clinical Molecular Genetics and Clinical Cytogenetics

2)Houda Zghal Elloumi,. PhD, FACMG Associate Director, Clinical Genetics Lead, Genomics Analysis GeneDx | GeneDx.com

3. Rebecca Moore, MS Genomic Variant Scientist Variant IQTM – Variant Investigation by Quest Diagnostics

Response#2: Plan for getting gene curations approved:

Step 1: The plan will largely adhere to the suggested variant curation process in the ClinGen CMT VCEP SOP.

Step 2: Current members of the CMT GCEP will constitute the membership of CMT VCEP except the addition of Dr Sat Dev Batish, Ph.D., FACMGG, FAAN.

Step 3: The CMT VCEP will meet bi-monthly to discuss the latest variant classification proposals.

Step 4: Curations will closely adhere to the criteria set forth by the ACMG variant classification scheme.

Step 5: Based on review of multiple variants in any of the targeted genes, CMT VCEP will develop a modified variant curation protocol. This modified protocol will attempt to maximize the number of variants that can be classified either as benign or pathogenic and if possible, reduce the number of VUSs.

Step 6: We will use detailed information such as pedigree analyses, established biochemical biomarkers, functional evidence and more to improve beyond current classification standards in these genes.

Step 7: This curation process is iterative in nature as more complete picture of gene-specific variants and their detailed annotation becomes apparent.

Step 8: Final approval of our curation results and adjusted gene-specific ACMG criteria will be obtained from the CDWG OC Chairs/Committee.

Response #3:Plan for reconciling what disease entities will be curated on:

Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (also known as Charcot-Marie Tooth disease) phenotypes will be the primary phenotype associated with the specific gene and corresponding variant chosen for curation. All Mondo IDs used for the gene curations will be the exact ones used for variant curations. The CMT VCEP members include some of the key clinical thought leaders for these phenotypes. It is widely recognized that phenotypic variability of these clinical entities is not optimally represented in ANY classification scheme including MONDO. Examples are the clinical and genetic overlaps between Hereditary Sensory and Motor Neuropathy/Hereditary Motor Neuropathy and more complexed forms of peripheral neuropathies.

Summary of the main reasons for choosing these three genes:

Of the more than 100 genes associated with CMT, the most common and well-known being PMP22, we have chosen three genes for variant curation based on the potential for therapeutic management, MFN2 (Mitofusin 2), GDAP1(ganglioside-induced differentiation-associated protein 1) and SORD (sorbitol dehydrogenase).

Presently, there are several therapeutic developments in process for Mitofusin 2-related CMT. It is by far the most common axonal form of CMT with reasonably high polymorphic allelic heterogeneity, (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30830587/) (https://beta.clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04881201).  
GDAP1-related CMT is an unusually severe form of CMT and one of the most common autosomal recessive forms with several therapeutic developments in process, (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34274972/),(https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34440148/).  
SORD-related CMT is estimated to affect more than 60,000 persons worldwide, likely the most common autosomal recessive form of CMT, with active enrollment in a phase III clinical trial (https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT05397665). Furthermore, the increasing number of variants of unknown significance associated with SORD will require expedited resolution to enable therapeutic engagement in the active clinical trials.

Main points about each gene:

SORD • Current ClinGen Classification: Definitive Evidence • Most common autosomal recessive form of CMT • Measurable biochemical marker with sorbitol level • Increasing number of VUSs that will need resolution • Enrolled/upcoming clinical trials/need to establish pathogenicity • MONDO:0030055

MFN2 • Current ClinGen Classification: Definitive Evidence • Most common CMT2 gene • High allelic heterogeneity • Severe forms; will likely be an urgent need to establish pathogenic variants for clinical trial readiness and family planning • MONDO:0018775

GDAP1 • Current ClinGen Classification: Definitive Evidence • Unusually severe CMT phenotype • A common form of recessive CMT • Therapeutic potential • MONDO: 0015626","membership_description":"Houda Elloumi-Clinical lab director with years of experience in the interpretation and appropriate use of ACMG/AMP guidelines Rebecca Moore-Clinical lab director with years of experience in the interpretation and appropriate use of ACMG/AMP guidelines Kumarie Latchman-Clinical geneticist with more than 4 years of experience in the interpretation and application of ACMG/AMP guidelines Steve Scherer-Neurologist with expertise in CMT and other peripheral neuropathies as well as significant experience in the interpretation and application of ACMG/AMP guidelines Tanya Stojkovic-Neurologist with expertise in CMT and other peripheral neuropathies as well as significant experience in the interpretation and application of ACMG/AMP guidelines","type":"vcep","date_completed":null,"inactive_date":null,"current_step":3,"all_genes":[{"hgnc_id":11184,"gene_symbol":"SORD","mondo_id":"MONDO:0030055","disease_name":"sorbitol dehydrogenase deficiency with peripheral neuropathy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":16877,"gene_symbol":"MFN2","mondo_id":"MONDO:0012231","disease_name":"Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 2A2","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":15968,"gene_symbol":"GDAP1","mondo_id":"MONDO:0008961","disease_name":"Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 4A","disease_entity":null}],"clinvar_org_id":null,"vcep_definition_approval":"2023-05-09T04:00:00.000000Z","vcep_draft_specification_approval":"2024-07-10T04:00:00.000000Z","vcep_pilot_approval":null,"vcep_final_approval":null},"parent":{"uuid":"422c495c-a77d-4a70-aa60-2bee7d0ca980","name":"Neurological Disorders","type":"cdwg","status":"active"}}}

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:54","data":{"uuid":"422c495c-a77d-4a70-aa60-2bee7d0ca980","name":"Neurological Disorders","description":"The Neurological Disorders Clinical Domain Working Group (ND CDWG) aims to create a comprehensive, standardized knowledge base of genes and variants relevant for neurological disorders, genetics, and genomic medicine. This initial effort will encompass disorders typically seen by neuromuscular, cognitive, and movement disorder specialists. This will expand over time to include other areas such as headache and cerebrovascular disorders, ataxia, hereditary spastic paraplegia, and dystonia.

As neurological disorders cross multiple traditional phenotypic boundaries, the group aims to collaborate with existing ClinGen CDWGs and Expert Panels, to harmonize and expand approaches to gene curation, variant curation, and phenotype determination across neurological disorders. This effort is therefore connected to but distinct from, for example, the Neurodevelopmental Clinical Domain Working Group that focuses on brain malformations, cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, epilepsy, etc.

Due to overlap in expertise and scope of work, the [Neuromuscular Disorders CDWG](https://clinicalgenome.org/working-groups/clinical-domain/neuromuscular/) and the [Neurodegenerative CDWG](https://clinicalgenome.org/working-groups/clinical-domain/neurodegenerative-cdwg/) were merged in January 2023 to create the Neurological Disorders CDWG.

**Goals**

1. Identify neurological disorders experts and clinical labs willing to disseminate knowledge for gene and variant curation efforts.
2. Identify and recruit domain experts willing and able to participate in projects including, but not limited to, gene and variant curation.
3. Curate and adjudicate genes associated with neuromuscular, cognitive, movement, etc. disorders using the ClinGen Gene Curation Working Group gene:disease classification framework.
4. Specify ACMG/AMP guidelines for a given set of genes, and curate variants within those genes to determine their pathogenicity.
5. Define the phenotypic and other data needed to assess pathogenicity (e.g., functional assays, histopathology, etc.).
6. Establish new expert panels to address key neuromuscular and neurodegenerative disease areas not already represented in other CDWGs (e.g., myopathies, LGMD, CMT, primary dementias, inherited ataxias, etc.).
7. Establish strong working relationships with CDWGs, working groups, and expert panels with overlapping scope of work internal and external to the ClinGen consortium.
8. Widely disseminate work products in publicly accessible forums, including through ClinVar, ClinGen, and peer-reviewed {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:53","data":{"uuid":"15e2cf4a-b060-49ce-94ee-967aff1deaf3","name":"Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase VCEP","description":"Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is an X-linked genetic condition affecting an estimated 400 to 500 million people worldwide and is the most common genetic cause of chronic hemolytic anemia and drug-, food-, or infection-induced hemolytic anemia. Most G6PD-deficient individuals are asymptomatic, unless they are exposed to a triggering event (e.g., fava beans, certain drugs). When this happens, the result can be life-threatening with the most common clinical manifestation being acute hemolytic anemia, which is characterized by fatigue, back pain, anemia, and jaundice. In rare cases, individuals with the most severe genotype of G6PD deficiency exhibit chronic nonspherocytic hemolytic anemia (CNSHA) in the absence of any triggering agents. A Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) guideline exists for guiding medication use in the context of G6PD deficiency. In individuals with G6PD deficiency, high-risk medications should be avoided, medium-risk medications should be used with caution, and low-to-no risk medications can be used with standard precautions, without regard to G6PD phenotype.

In pursuit of providing high quality and systematic curation of G6PD variants, we have assembled a panel of clinical, academic, and industry experts in the fields of genetics, pharmacogenomics, hematology, functional genetics, and biochemistry. With research progressing, determining the clinical impact of G6PD variants is critical to ongoing efforts towards diagnosis and patient care. Curation efforts will synergize with ongoing functional studies, analyses of function/genotype/phenotype correlations, and efforts to guide pharmacotherapy recommendations.

dehydrogenase","short_name":"G6PD","scope_description":"Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is an X-linked genetic condition affecting an estimated 400 to 500 million people worldwide and is the most common genetic cause of chronic hemolytic anemia and drug-, food-, or infection-induced hemolytic anemia. Most G6PD-deficient patients are asymptomatic, but G6PD deficiency can be life-threatening with the most common clinical manifestations of G6PD deficiency being neonatal jaundice and acute hemolytic anemia, which is characterized by fatigue, back pain, anemia, and jaundice. Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) guidelines exist for guiding medication use in the context of G6PD deficiency. In patients with G6PD deficiency, high-risk medications should be avoided, medium-risk medications should be used with caution, and low-to-no risk medications can be used with standard precautions, without regard to G6PD phenotype.

The diagnosis of G6PD deficiency can be made by testing G6PD activity levels in erythrocytes or by the identification of genetic variants in the gene G6PD that are known to be associated with disease. Phenotypic G6PD diagnostics must be interpreted cautiously due to a number of hematologic influences and intra-individual variability, as well as variability between labs and assays in activity measures and reference ranges. Human G6PD is encoded by the G6PD gene, which is in a single copy on the long arm of chromosome X (Xq28). The full gene spans 16 kb and comprises 13 exons. Over 1,300 variants in G6PD have been described in the literature, and variants can be found alone or in combination on the same chromosome, both of which are referred to as G6PD alleles. Clinical manifestations are predominantly observed in individuals with low residual enzymatic activity who are either homozygous or hemizygous for disease-associated G6PD alleles; individuals harboring a heterozygous disease-associated G6PD allele can have clinical manifestation when their residual enzymatic activity is low.

G6PD alleles have historically been divided into classes by the World Health Organization (WHO) based on the level of G6PD activity in erythrocytes and the clinical presentation of individuals bearing hemizygous or homozygous alleles. In 2022, the WHO updated the classification system to include four classes: A (< 20% of G6PD activity/chronic hemolysis); B (< 45% of G6PD activity/acute, triggered hemolysis); C (60-150% of G6PD activity/no hemolysis); and U (any G6PD activity/uncertain clinical significance).

Several public databases (ClinVar, CPIC, Human Gene Mutation Database, and Leiden Open Variation Database) list more than 400 different G6PD variants with functional evidence. Unfortunately, many variants are still listed as VUS.

In pursuit of providing high quality and systematic curation of G6PD variants, we have assembled a panel of clinical, academic, and industry experts in the fields of genetics, pharmacogenomics, hematology, functional genetics, and biochemistry. With research progressing, determining the functional activity and/or pathogenicity of variants is critical to ongoing efforts towards diagnosis and patient care. Curation efforts will synergize with ongoing functional studies, analyses of function/ genotype/phenotype correlations, and efforts to guide medication precautions.","membership_description":"There are three individuals on the VCEP who perform clinical lab case sign-out: Vimla Aggarwal (Clinical Molecular Geneticist), Germana Bancone (Research Scientist), and Angelo Minucci (Molecular Biologist).","type":"vcep","date_completed":null,"inactive_date":null,"current_step":3,"all_genes":[{"hgnc_id":4057,"gene_symbol":"G6PD","mondo_id":"MONDO:0005775","disease_name":"G6PD deficiency","disease_entity":null}],"clinvar_org_id":null,"vcep_definition_approval":"2023-05-01T04:00:00.000000Z","vcep_draft_specification_approval":"2025-02-25T12:25:10.000000Z","vcep_pilot_approval":null,"vcep_final_approval":null},"parent":{"uuid":"2e312275-a400-47ab-a34c-cad7698703cf","name":"Inborn Errors Metabolism","type":"cdwg","status":"active"}}}

Autoinflammatory Diseases","short_name":"SAIDs","scope_description":"Monogenic autoinflammatory diseases or systemic autoinflammatory diseases (SAIDs) are disorders of the innate immune system characterized by pathogenic variants in a single gene. Symptoms of these conditions can include recurrent fevers, rashes, arthritis/arthralgia, myalgia, GI inflammation neurological features, elevated acute phase reactants, immunodeficiency, bone marrow failure and more. The initial gene list was drafted to include genes included on the International Union of Immunological Societies (IUIS) Autoinflammatory List (Table 7) (PMID32048120) and genes that are included on commercially available NGS panels. We expect that this list will grow as the field advances. Some of the genes on our list overlap with the lists of other GCEPs, including the Primary Immune Regulatory Disorders (PIRD) GCEP. In these cases, we will communicate and collaborate with the other GCEPs on final curations.

Responses to Feedback provided on Apr 6, 2023:

We have reorganized the membership so that each member is only listed once. We have a plan to add additional personnel. 1) We are reaching out to other GCEPs to solicit volunteers with expertise in applying the ClinGen Gene Curation Framework. 2) We will use the C3 Volunteer program to recruit additional biocurators and have added the GCEP to the spreadsheet of EPs accepting volunteers. 3) We will also ask our experts to recruit their junior colleagues to join the group as biocurators.

The immune dysregulatory disorders or PIRDs are monogenic disorders that are associated loss of inflammatory control and normal mechanisms of immune tolerance with autoimmunity, lymphoproliferation, aberrant lymphocyte cytotoxicity and impaired control of viral (e.g. EBV) infections as predominant features. The PIRD GCEP is organized under the auspices of the Immunology CDWG. The experts and biocurators selected have familiarity with gene curation of immunology genes. Three of the genes (CTLA4, LRBA, IKZF1) in this list have already been curated by the Antibody Defects GCEP and will include a statement to reflect the prior curation. The PIRD genes are relevant to curate as this is a growing group of the inborn errors of immunity (IEIs) and several of them have molecularly targeted therapeutics available, which can be very effective for disease management. The Immunology CDWG leadership has had recent discussions with the RAD (Rheumatologic and Autoimmune disorders) CDWG and identified areas of collaboration. The RAD CDWG - Autoinflammatory GCEP (Co-Chairs:¨Drs. Ivona Aksentijevich and Michael Ombrello) will commence gene curation on Table VII (Autoinflammatory IEIs) of the IUIS classification. The PIRD GCEP is proposing to perform gene curation on Table IV of the IUIS classification. Any genes with overlapping interests will be jointly curated by the Autoinflammatory GCEP of the RAD CDWG and the PIRD {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:51","data":{"uuid":"327970de-6354-4026-84d1-3103518e4e21","name":"Galactosemia","description":"The Galactosemia Variant Curation Expert Panel plans to curate, in the order of clinical importance, the variants of four galactosemia-associated genes with a “strong” or “definitive” gene disease validity, starting with GALT. The Inborn Errors of Metabolism Gene Curation Expert Panel (IEM GCEP) curated GALE, GALK1, and GALT to have a “definitive” gene disease validity, and GALM to have a “strong” gene disease validity. Current members of the expert panel are experienced in different aspects of galactosemia and inborn errors of metabolism, including direct patient care, biochemical and molecular laboratory interpretation and testing, and variant classification/curation.

Variant interpretation criteria will be adapted and specified for each gene, particularly PVS1, PM3, PS2, PS3, PM2, BA1, BS1, PP3/BP4, PM5 and PP4, similarly to prior variant curation procedures for inborn errors of metabolism genes. A curation SOP will be created for standardization and the ClinGen Variant Curation Interface (VCI) will be used to document evidence used for variant classification.

Variants in the selected genes will be collected from the Human Gene Mutation Database (HGMD -http://www.hgmd.cf.ac.uk/), ARUP Galactosemia database (PMID 17486650), GalNet (PMID 27837294), and Leiden Open Variation Database (LVOD -http://www.lovd.nl/3.0/home) and ClinVar. Additional variants are expected to be identified by the VCEP members by other means as well, including (but not limited to) existing molecular testing laboratories and metabolic clinics/clinicians. are inborn errors of galactose metabolism, some of which are routinely detected by newborn screening due to elevations in total galactose levels in plasma and/or decreased GALT enzyme activity (PMID 2748263, 6071286, 11919338, 12552079, 14233158).

Galactose is a natural sugar that is released during the digestion of lactose (milk sugar) to glucose and galactose in the intestine. Galactose is also synthesized endogenously at significant levels, especially in infants and children. Defects in the galactose pathway, aka Leloir pathway, lead to accumulation of biochemical compounds or side products, which are believed to cause a wide spectrum of clinical manifestations (PMID 35882174). Classic galactosemia, the most severe and common condition in this pathway, presents following milk exposure with jaundice, failure to thrive, cataracts, acute liver failure, and E.coli sepsis. Even with the benefit of early detection and rigorous dietary restriction of galactose, many patients grow to experience developmental delay and premature ovarian failure, among other complications. The other forms of galactosemia tend to be comparatively milder, or extremely rare (generalized GALE deficiency) and may present with elevated metabolites and cataracts with or without liver involvement, failure to thrive and neurodevelopmental symptoms. In general, restriction of galactose intake, and therefore lactose intake, forms the basis of life-long therapy for galactosemia.

There are currently 4 enzymes in the Leloir pathway that have been associated with human disease or phenotype. These are, galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT, EC 2.7.7.12) (PMID 20301691, 25473725), galactokinase (GALK1, EC 2.7.1.6) (PMID 7670469, 10570908), UDP-galactose-4’-epimerase (GALE, EC 5.1.3.2) (PMID 21290786), and galactose mutarotase (or aldose 1-epimerase) (GALM, EC 5.1.3.3) (PMID 30451973).

In addition to elevated total blood galactose levels, other biochemical parameters, including Gal-1-P, galactitol and galactonic acid levels, and individual enzyme activities, help both diagnose and distinguish the different types of galactosemia. The biochemical profiles and the clinical presentation of each galactosemia varies depending on the affected enzyme and the degree of catalytic deficit.

The Galactosemia Variant Curation Expert Panel plans to curate, in the order of clinical importance, the variants of all galactosemia associated genes with a “strong” or “definitive” gene disease validity, starting with GALT and GALK1. The Inborn Errors of Metabolism Gene Curation Expert Panel (IEM GCEP) has currently curated 2 genes, GALT and GALK1, to have a “definitive” gene disease validity. The remaining two genes (GALE, GALM) have not been curated by the IEM GCEP, but a request was submitted to the IEM GCEP by this Galactosemia VCEP in October 2022. Once the curation of the remaining two genes has been completed, we will submit an addendum to this SOW to include any additional genes with “strong” or “definitive” gene disease validity. Variant interpretation criteria will be adapted and specified for each gene, particularly PVS1, PM3, PS2, PS3, PM2, BA1, BS1, PP3/BP4, PM5 and PP4, similarly to prior variant curation procedures for inborn errors of metabolism. A curation SOP will be created for standardization and the ClinGen Variant Curation Interface (VCI) will be used to document evidence used for variant classification.

Variants in the selected genes will be collected from the Human Gene Mutation Database (HGMD -http://www.hgmd.cf.ac.uk/), ARUP Galactosemia database (PMID 17486650), GalNet (PMID 27837294), and Leiden Open Variation Database (LVOD -http://www.lovd.nl/3.0/home) and ClinVar. Additional variants are expected to be identified through a collection of variants, which have been observed in contributing molecular testing laboratories and by participating metabolic clinics/clinicians. Currently ClinVar lists approximately 559 variants in GALT and 293 variants in the GALK1 gene (accessed: 11/21/2022). Members of the current expert panel are experienced in different aspects of galactosemia and inborn errors of metabolism, including direct patient care, biochemical and molecular laboratory interpretation and testing, and variant classification/curation.

Disorders of Glycosylation","short_name":"CDG","scope_description":"The Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation (CDG) GCEP will use the ClinGen Gene-Disease Clinical Validity framework to assess clinical validity for genes involved in disorders of glycosylation including other genes related to or considered secondary disorders of glycosylation and those genes related to abnormal patterns of glycosylation. The experts in the CDG GCEP are extensively knowledgeable in both the clinical and research realm of metabolic disease with particular expertise in glycosylation genes included in this GCEP. Some members of the group participate in other ClinGen GCEPs and therefore, already have a working knowledge of the gene-disease clinical validity framework. The members of this group are varied experts in the field of glycosylation in both the clinical and research realm. This group will consult the ClinGen Gene Curation WG and the Lumping and Splitting WG if questions regarding lumping and splitting arise while curating CDG and related genes.

The proposed gene list has been compiled by CDG researchers and physicians affiliated with the Rare Disease Clinical Research Network (RDCRN) Frontiers in CDG Consortium (FCDGC). It was influenced by this publication: PMCID: PMC9021760, PMID: 33340416. Additionally, this list was cross referenced and collated from the larger gene panel currently available from Invitae. Additional genes involved in glycosylation may be added in the future, as this is a quickly and rapidly expanding field. All 188 genes on the initial list agreed upon by our experts were searched on the ClinGen website to determine if gene-disease clinical validity had already been performed and, if so, for which disease entity/entities. 50 Gene-disease pairs that have already been curated by other ClinGen GCEPs (Table 2) have been removed from the list. A search was also performed to identify other GCEPs which may be interested in the same genes. The coordinators of those GCEPs have been contacted to discuss plans for curation (see Table 1 footnotes, and Table 2). The current proposed gene list (Table 1) includes the 138 genes that the CDG GCEP plans to curate. We recognize that some genes on the list may be involved in more than one disorder. The ClinGen Lumping and Splitting guidelines will be followed to make decisions regarding the appropriate disease entity/entities for curation.

Prioritization of genes and rationale: We decided as an expert panel to start the curations with the following genes: PMM2, PGM1, STT3A, FUT8, and COG4. These genes, which are well known in their disease pathogenesis in the CDG clinical and research literature, will provide us with the information needed to pinpoint disease-specific considerations when implementing the clinical validity framework. Genes will then be curated as selected by curators based on flow of work. We will also review some genes that have been previously curated by other GCEPs to assess whether it may be beneficial to include additional details on their role in glycosylation.

Footnotes to the gene list: a Curated by the General IEM GCEP, Sept 2022. Will be recurated by the CDG GCEP in Sept 2024. b Contact Cardiovascular GCEP prior to curation. c Prenatal GCEP willing to assist with prenatal phenotypes. d Glomerulopathy GCEP willing to assist with renal phenotypes. e Send curation summary to SCID GCEP for input prior to approval.

The causes of CHD include chromosomal aneuploidies, copy number variations, exposure to environmental factors, and pathogenic variation in single genes.

The Congenital Heart Disease Expert Panel will develop a framework for determining the clinical validity of gene-disease relationships for CHD. The Congenital Heart Disease Expert Panel will curate and review genes for isolated and syndromic CHD that do not currently come under the purview of another GCEP. For genes that currently come under the purview of another GCEP, we will communicate and collaborate with the additional GCEP(s) to decide if a split curation is warranted. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:42","data":{"uuid":"2120e900-9d77-462c-ad9b-bbb72397a8c8","name":"Motile Ciliopathy","description":"Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) is a rare genetic condition belonging to the class of motile ciliopathies and is caused by abnormal structure, function, and biogenesis of motile cilia, a complex organelle present on the majority of the human cell surfaces. Ciliary abnormalities lead to the wide spectrum of symptoms manifested in various organ systems, resulting in neonatal respiratory distress in the term neonates, chronic oto-sino-pulmonary disease, organ laterality defects, infertility in males, and subfertility in females. PCD can be diagnosed by clinical phenotype, ciliary ultrastructural analysis, and molecular analysis. However, diagnosis can be challenging due to an overlap of clinical phenotype with other diseases, such as cystic fibrosis and primary immunodeficiency, variability of ciliary ultrastructure, which tends to be normal in ~30% of the cases, and extensive genetic heterogeneity. Currently, over 45 genes have been associated with PCD and motile ciliopathies, which are being curated under the auspices of the ClinGen Motile Ciliopathy GCEP. But in order to aid with the molecular diagnosis, it becomes pertinent to classify the variants in the curated genes such that it helps with the genetic diagnosis, and hence this VCEP has been established.

The first objective of the VCEP is the adaptation and validation of the ACMG/AMP standards and guidelines for interpreting sequence variants (Richards et al., 2015) for *DNAH5* variations, the most frequent etiology PCD. This will be carried out in three stages: (1) a systematic review of all rules and the development of proposed adjustments approved by the VCEP, (2) testing of the proposed rules using a representative set of at least 40 *DNAH5* variants and (3) refining rule adjustments as needed based on scenarios encountered during the testing phase.

The second objective is to classify variants reported in databases (ClinVar, OMIM) and/or the literature for *DNAH5*. Variant classification will focus on variants with conflicting interpretation in ClinVar while prioritizing the most common genes and variants associated with PCD.

In the future, the VCEP will develop and validate variant classification rules to curate variants in other PCD genes whose loss-of-function, like *DNAH5*, also corresponds to an absence of or defects in outer dynein arms (*DNAI1, DNAI2, ODAD1, ODAD2, ODAD3, ODAD4*). The VCEP will focus only on genes with strong or definitive evidence that variation causes PCD, as determined by the Motile Ciliopathy Ciliopathy","short_name":"Motile Cilia","scope_description":"Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) is a rare genetic condition belonging to the class of motile ciliopathies and is caused by abnormal structure, function, and biogenesis of motile cilia, a complex organelle present on the majority of the human cell surfaces. Ciliary abnormalities lead to the wide spectrum of symptoms manifested in various organ systems, resulting in neonatal respiratory distress in the term neonates, chronic oto-sino-pulmonary disease, organ laterality defects, infertility in males, and subfertility in females. PCD can be diagnosed by clinical phenotype, ciliary ultrastructural analysis, and molecular analysis. However, diagnosis can be challenging due to an overlap of clinical phenotype with other diseases, such as cystic fibrosis and primary immunodeficiency, variability of ciliary ultrastructure, which tends to be normal in ~30% of the cases, and extensive genetic heterogeneity. Currently, over 45 genes have been associated with PCD and motile ciliopathies, which are being curated under the auspices of the ClinGen Motile Ciliopathy GCEP. But in order to aid with the molecular diagnosis, it becomes pertinent to classify the variants in the curated genes such that it helps with the genetic diagnosis, and hence this VCEP has been established.

The first objective of the VCEP is the adaptation and validation of the ACMG/AMP standards and guidelines for interpreting sequence variants (Richards et al., 2015) for DNAH5 variations, the most frequent etiology PCD. This will be carried out in three stages: (1) a systematic review of all rules and the development of proposed adjustments approved by the VCEP, (2) testing of the proposed rules using a representative set of at least 40 DNAH5 variants and (3) refining rule adjustments as needed based on scenarios encountered during the testing phase.

The second objective is to classify variants reported in databases (ClinVar, OMIM) and/or the literature for DNAH5. Variant classification will focus on variants with conflicting interpretation in ClinVar while prioritizing the most common genes and variants associated with PCD.

In the future, the VCEP will develop and validate variant classification rules to curate variants in other PCD genes whose loss-of-function, like DNAH5, also corresponds to an absence of or defects in outer dynein arms (DNAI1, DNAI2, ODAD1, ODAD2, ODAD3, ODAD4). The pediatric manifestations include the rare set of childhood ILDs (chILD) characterized by remodeling of lung parenchyma leading to abnormal gas exchange. These have been classified broadly into those that occur during infancy (<2 years of age) and those that are not specific to infancy (>2 years of age). The childhood presentations are generally distinct from the presentations of ILD in older adults. Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF) is the prototypic disease characterized by progressive lung fibrosis disease affecting adults. Overall, it is considered a rare disease, affecting about 5-20 per 100,000 individuals. However, in populations older than 65 years of age, there is a much higher prevalence, up to 0.5%. Progression of lung fibrosis is a defining characteristic of IPF, even though the inter-individual rate of progression is variable. Genetic pulmonary fibrosis is most often diagnosed as IPF, but other forms of progressive pulmonary fibrosis are also common.

Studies show that rare variant risk alleles are found at a higher frequency in patients with a family history of pulmonary fibrosis than those with sporadic presentations. The risk factors have been shown in patients with a wide phenotypic spectrum of pulmonary fibrosis, both in children and in adults. The inheritance pattern of disease relates to the gene defect. For example, surfactant protein deficiency due to mutations in SFTPB or ABCA3 present as an autosomal recessive disorder. Surfactant protein deficiency due to mutations in SFTPC presents as an autosomal dominant disorder, with lung disease spanning the full age spectrum, from infancy to older adults. Lung disease is part of the spectrum of clinical phenotypes associated with dyskeratosis congenita and the short telomere syndromes. The X-linked form of dyskeratosis congenita is due to mutations in DKC1. Autosomal dominant and autosomal recessive presentations of dyskeratosis congenita with pulmonary fibrosis have been described. Most older adults with a family history of ILD present with an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance with incomplete penetrance, although exceptions have been published in case reports. In these subjects, pulmonary fibrosis can be the only manifestation of the short telomere syndrome.

There are difficulties in the field, regarding phenotypic variability, tenuous gene-disease links, and questionable pathogenicity of genetic variants. Hence, there is a need for ClinGen-based gene curation. As for many lung presentations of genetic disease, ILD manifests as a disease with variable penetrance, often associated with non-pulmonary phenotypes. Disease characteristics rely upon qualitative assessment of chest radiographs. There may be an increasing reliance on genetic testing and interpretation. It is currently accepted that there are about 35 ILD-causing genes. Current NGS methods can diagnose around 10-25% of cases with causal mutations, hence we expect more genes to emerge. New publications have reported increasingly smaller patient numbers and harder to interpret gene variants. The expert team we have gathered has extensive knowledge about this variable penetrance, multisystem disease. Much remains unknown about genotype-phenotype relationships.

We plan to utilize the ClinGen Lumping and Splitting guidance in order to avoid splitting our analysis into multiple phenotypes. We will employ Gene Tracker to enter the precuration data on a selected expert list of around 45 genes that we have compiled and we welcome ClinGen’s expert opinion and feedback on our current gene list.

***ILD GCEP Application Updates***

Five individuals who have expertise in the field of telomere syndrome biology and in manifestation of telopathies in lung disease have agreed to join the ILD GCEP: Jonathan Alder, Paul Wolters, Ivan Rosas, and both chairs, Christine Garcia, and Coline van Moorsel.

Sharon Savage and Allison Bertush both accepted invitations to join the ILD GCEP as experts. They are interested in developing a telomere biology disorder/telomeropathy GCEP and VCEP and considering a U24 application. They stated that they would like to collaborate with the ILD GCEP, as well as the Myeloid Malignancy VCEP, on genes of shared interest. The latter EP is interested in adding telomere biology disorder genes to their scope of work.

The ILD GCEP will collaborate with existing and future ClinGen GCEPs for any curations involving syndromic diseases or are of shared interest with other EPs.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The chairs would like the GCEP's short name to remain as 'ILD GCEP' as it is commonly used in the field and is most inclusive of and specific to the group's scope of work.

goal of this VCEP is to curate clinically relevant variants in the ABCA4 gene, which is a 50 exon gene that has been associated with multiple phenotypes that deserves the full attention of a single VCEP. The ABCA4 gene spans 128kb and encodes a 2273 amino acid protein that removes N-retinylidene-PE, a waste product made during phototransduction, from photoreceptor cells in the retina. This gene has been associated with several inherited retinal dystrophies, such as Stargardt disease, retinitis pigmentosa, cone-rod dystrophy and other inherited maculopathies. Typically, diseases associated with ABCA4 variants are associated with recessive modes. At this time, the VCEP plans to focus its curation on autosomal recessive maculopathies as a result of biallelic pathogenic ABCA4 variants. The ClinGen Retina GCEP has reviewed the ABCA4 gene and decided to lump all recessive forms of retinopathies into one group of ABCA4-related retinopathies, which reached a definitive gene/disease classification.

More than 2,200 variants have been reported in the ABCA4 gene (LOVD ABCA4 database). Currently in ClinVar, there are 240 variants with conflicting classifications and 816 variants of uncertain significance. The ABCA4 gene is thought to account for 2-5% of autosomal recessive retinitis pigmentosa, and there have been 100+ genes associated with this clinical diagnosis. A large percentage of ABCA4 related retinopathy is diagnosed in a patient population often referred to as having a clinical diagnosis of Stargardt disease (PMID: 32278709), which has approximately 1 in 8,000 disease incidence. However, only approximately two-thirds of Stargardt patients will have two biallelic pathogenic variants identified on routine gene sequencing, with the other third often only harboring one deleterious ABCA4 variant (PMID: 32278709). This phenomenon is thought to be due in part to the high number of intronic variants that are suspected to be disease causing and missed on routine sequencing assays (PMID: 32307445). This further complicates the work of this VCEP to manage curation of a large number of potentially disease causing intronic variants that have been identified either in clinical or research laboratories. Additionally, there are several known modifier variants with higher minor allele frequencies (MAF) that can be difficult to curate as well, and there are several more common variants that were initially thought to be benign due to higher MAFs in population databases, but now are thought to be hypomorphic alleles that may only confer disease when in trans with a more severe ABCA4 deleterious variant. Other complex alleles have also been reported. These types of variants will require more time and collaboration with the ClinGen Low Penetrance/Risk Allele Working Group to appropriately classify.

ABCA4-related retinopathies are currently being treated with gene therapy, stem cell and/or optogenetics products in Phase I/II and III trials, which usually require at least one pathogenic variant in the ABCA4 gene. Therefore, there is an urgency to curate variants in this gene to establish eligibility for these clinical trials and potential future treatments.

Given the high complexity of the variants in this gene and the progress of gene therapy in clinical trials, we would like to only focus on the ABCA4 gene for curation, and we have recruited a set of highly knowledgeable and respected experts that devote a large amount of effort to understanding this important player in inherited retinal dystrophies.","membership_description":"We have three US-based clinical lab directors and one US-based clinical variant curation lab genetic counselor from four different clinical labs with expertise in retinal genetics. Additionally, we have four international molecular geneticists involved with research and/or clinical laboratories. We also have a genetic counselor coordinating this VCEP with clinical and variant curation experience with the ABCA4 gene, as well as expertise in ClinGen, and a ClinGen senior biocurator to assist with this work.","type":"vcep","date_completed":"2025-10-13T04:00:00.000000Z","inactive_date":null,"current_step":4,"all_genes":[{"hgnc_id":34,"gene_symbol":"ABCA4","mondo_id":"MONDO:0011137","disease_name":"retinitis pigmentosa 19","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":34,"gene_symbol":"ABCA4","mondo_id":"MONDO:0019353","disease_name":"Stargardt disease","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":34,"gene_symbol":"ABCA4","mondo_id":"MONDO:0011395","disease_name":"cone-rod dystrophy 3","disease_entity":null}],"clinvar_org_id":"510325","vcep_definition_approval":"2023-01-17T05:00:00.000000Z","vcep_draft_specification_approval":"2024-07-22T04:00:00.000000Z","vcep_pilot_approval":"2025-09-04T12:47:56.000000Z","vcep_final_approval":"2025-10-13T04:00:00.000000Z"},"parent":{"uuid":"eb3c04eb-8935-4d70-8705-d7aea5aec54a","name":"Ocular","type":"cdwg","status":"active"}}}

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:40","data":{"uuid":"bcee9c2f-b97c-4598-b0b9-fd632df20029","name":"Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis Spectrum Disorders","description":"The ALS Variant Curation Expert Panel aims to evaluate and classify the pathogenicity of variants in genes linked to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis spectrum disorders. The VCEP will develop and validate variant classification rules based on gene- and disease-specific modifications of the ACMG/AMP standards and guidelines for interpreting sequence variants (Richards et al., 2015). The ALS VCEP will begin with the curation of variants in *SOD1*, followed by *FUS*, as mutations in these genes are among the most common genetic causes for ALS, and there are gene-directed therapies already in clinical trial. In the future, the VCEP will develop and validate variant classification rules to curate variants in other genes that are relatively common genetic causes for ALS: *TARDBP, TBK1, OPTN, KIF5A, UBQLN2, VCP,* and *PFN1*. The VCEP will focus only on genes with strong or definitive evidence that variation causes ALS.

Lateral Sclerosis Spectrum Disorders","short_name":"ALS","scope_description":"The ALS Variant Curation Expert Panel aims to evaluate and classify the pathogenicity of variants in genes linked to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis spectrum disorders. The VCEP will develop and validate variant classification rules based on gene- and disease-specific modifications of the ACMG/AMP standards and guidelines for interpreting sequence variants (Richards et al., 2015). The ALS VCEP will begin with the curation of variants in SOD1, followed by FUS, as mutations in these genes are among the most common genetic causes for ALS, and there are gene-directed therapies already in clinical trial. In the future, the VCEP will develop and validate variant classification rules to curate variants in other genes that are relatively common genetic causes for ALS: TARDBP, TBK1, OPTN, KIF5A, UBQLN2, VCP, and PFN1. The VCEP will focus only on genes with strong or definitive evidence that variation causes ALS.

For this purpose, we have assembled a diverse group of ALS gene experts from academia and industry who specialize in the treatment of ALS, the genetics of ALS, laboratory genetics, animal and cellular modeling, and computational genetics.","membership_description":"The ALS VCEP has at least 4 experts with substantial experience in classifying monogenic disease variants. All individuals are knowledgeable of the ACMG/AMP Guidelines for Sequence Interpretation and of the genetic diagnosis of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis spectrum disorders and/or or neurological disorders:

\*Luke Drury, PhD, DLM(ASCP), HCLD(ABB), MB(ASCP) is a human molecular geneticist and clinical laboratory director at PreventionGenetics. He is certified as a high complexity clinical lab director through the American Board of Bioanalysis and as a diplomat of laboratory management through the American Society of Clinical Pathology. He specializes in red blood cell disorders and ALS. He is also a member of the ALS GCEP.

\*Sali Farhan, PhD, FCCMG is a clinical molecular geneticist at the McGill University Health Centre. She is also an assistant professor in the Department of Neurology and Neurosurgery, and the Department of Human Genetics at McGill University. Her research focuses on the genetics of neurodegeneration, with a central focus on ALS, and she has contributed to the identification of several disease genes. She is also a member of the ALS GCEP.

\*Lijun Wang, FACMG, MD, PhD, DABMGG is director of the Vanderbilt Clinical Genomics Laboratory and an assistant professor of pathology, microbiology and immunology at Vanderbilt University. He has authored several research papers focused on ALS and the gene SOD1. He is also a current member of the Antibody Deficiencies VCEP.

\*Yuan Xue, MS, PhD, FACMG is a laboratory director at Fulgent Genetics and an adjunct instructor in the Department of Human Genetics at Emory University. Her areas of specialization include molecular genetic diagnostic testing, molecular diagnostic testing for skeletal dysplasias, and the FGFRe signaling pathway.

*NOTES*\*Please note that Dr. Yuan Xue has accepted an invitation to join the ALS VCEP from the coordinator and chair via email and confirmed that she is willing to complete a COI survey. Her name in the GPM has been removed for now so as not to delay the review of the Step 1 application.

\*Additional experts who use the ACMG/AMP guidelines to classify variants and/or review variants during clinical laboratory case sign-out are being recruited from industry (Invitae, Genentech, Illumina, etc.) and academia. Existing ClinGen consortium members have been asked for colleagues who may be potentially interested in joining. Advertising to recruit additional individuals will also be done at upcoming professional society meetings & conferences.

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:39","data":{"uuid":"b765c7fb-b91d-4afc-9995-d07338bd45d5","name":"Monogenic Systemic and Incomplete Lupus Erythematosus","description":"The aim of the Monogenic Systemic and Incomplete Lupus Erythematosus GCEP will be to curate the validity of gene-disease associations in the context of clinical phenotypes consistent with lupus. Lupus is a chronic multisystem and phenotypically heterogeneous autoimmune disease. Loss of immune tolerance leads to the generation of autoreactive immune cells leading to inflammation and damage of multiple organs, including the skin, kidney, and CNS.

The diagnosis of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) is made based on clinical and laboratory findings, typically according to the three commonly used classification criteria (the ACR 1997, SLICC 2012, and ACR/EULAR 2019 classification criteria for SLE). Despite improvements over time, these criteria fail to identify all cases, and many patients, particularly at time of initial presentation, do not meet classification criteria and are described by terminology including “incomplete lupus” and “lupus-like” disease. Evidence strongly suggests a genetic component in lupus susceptibility, with over 50 genes having been postulated as potential causes of monogenic lupus or lupus-like disease, and additional gene variants described in association with lupus. However, the level of certainty and strength of gene-disease associations have not been well characterized.

Our gene list includes genes selected by expert opinion based on literature review and refined in discussion with members of the Lupus GCEP, the Monogenic Autoinflammatory GCEP leadership, and the Systemic and Incomplete Lupus Erythematosus","short_name":"Lupus","scope_description":"The aim of the Lupus GCEP will be to curate the validity of gene-disease associations in the context of clinical phenotypes consistent with lupus. Lupus is a chronic multisystem and phenotypically heterogeneous autoimmune disease. Loss of immune tolerance leads to the generation of autoreactive immune cells leading to inflammation and damage of multiple organs, including the skin, kidney, and CNS.

The diagnosis of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) is made based on clinical and laboratory findings, typically according to the three commonly used classification criteria (the ACR 1997, SLICC 2012, and ACR/EULAR 2019 classification criteria for SLE). Despite improvements over time, these criteria fail to identify all cases, and many patients, particularly at time of initial presentation, do not meet classification criteria and are described by terminology including “incomplete lupus” and “lupus-like” disease. Evidence strongly suggests a genetic component in lupus susceptibility, with over 50 genes having been postulated as potential causes of monogenic lupus or lupus-like disease, and additional gene variants described in association with lupus. However, the level of certainty and strength of gene-disease associations have not been well characterized.

Our gene list includes 55 genes, selected by expert opinion based on literature review (including PMIDs 30459768, 32151092, 35149194) and refined in discussion with members of the Lupus GCEP, the Monogenic Autoinflammatory GCEP leadership, and the broader RAD CDWG. We recognize that our lupus gene list overlaps with those of other Expert Panels, and we plan to communicate and collaborate with other GCEPs with shared interest.

Responses to feedback from 4.20.23 email: Requested Revisions: For the 11 genes on our list that have been previously curated by other GCEPs, we plan to follow ClinGen lumping and splitting rules to determine whether a separate curation for lupus is indicated, or whether alternative action in collaboration with the other GCEP, such as inclusion of lupus in the existing evidence summary, is appropriate.

The scope of this GCEP will include both patients with SLE as well as those with incomplete lupus (ILE), defined as those with ANA positivity and one or two additional SLE classification criteria.

A subset of the GCEP membership will also be collaborating with the multigenic framework taskforce, co-chaired by Tony Merriman and Adrienne Tin from the RAD CDWG. Any genes that are found in precuration to be inappropriate for monogenic gene-disease validity curation in the ClinGen framework will be moved to the list for later multigenic curation in pilot efforts.

Additional Suggestions: We plan to recruit additional biocurators, and are on the list of EPs accepting volunteers through C3. We also plan to recruit an additional expert with experience in the ClinGen framework, and are reaching out to Immunology and Syndromic Disorders GCEP co-chairs for a volunteer.

For better breadth and clarity, we have updated the full name of the GCEP to “Monogenic Systemic and Incomplete Lupus
