and Leukoencephalopathy","short_name":"Leuko","scope_description":"Leukodystrophies (LDs) are heritable disorders affecting the glial-axonal unit and resulting in differences in the genesis and maintenance of myelin. Despite many recent advances, the genetics of LDs remain incompletely characterized. With the recent emergence of broad use next-generation sequencing, over 240 genes affecting myelin have recently been identified.

As the community prepares for additional clinical trials, advocacy partners, clinicians and scientists have consolidated clinical trial readiness efforts in a Rare Disease Clinical Research Network (RDCRN) funded consortium, the Global LD Initiative (GLIA). GLIA has established a case description, using a modified Delphi approach, for the LDs. The expert consensus established that “LDs are heritable disorders affecting the white matter of the CNS with or without peripheral nervous system involvement. These disorders have in common glial cell or myelin sheath abnormalities…\[and\] neuropathology is primarily characterized by involvement of oligodendrocytes, astrocytes and other non-neuronal cell types,…, including significant axonal pathology.” In this definition, LDs were distinguished from other, secondary causes of white matter injury, including acquired and infectious etiologies, as well as primary epilepsies and developmental disorders. Based on this definition and exclusions, we have identified a list of all genes historically and recently associated with the LD phenotype via literature review and expert consensus. For more exact classification and organization, we have further divided the leukodystrophies into several subtypes: Demyelinating, Hypomyelinating, Myelin Vasculoization, Astrocytopathies, Leukoaxonopathies, Microgliopathies, Leukovasculopathies, and Unclassified.

We recognize that our leukodystrophy gene list overlaps with those of other Expert Panels. LDs may also be categorized in the Inborn Errors of Metabolism, including lysosomal disorders, peroxisomal disorders and mitochondrial disorders. Because of the considerable overlap, the LeukoGCEP chairs will regularly review curation activities with the chairs of other expert panels and collaborate as necessary. To date, our list of 231 genes includes 94 genes that also appear on other GCEP lists. We will contact the relevant GCEPs to ensure that the disease definitions they provide include a leukodystrophy phenotype, and then prioritize those that have not yet been curated for curation efforts.

(Please note: Per email exchanges with Erin Riggs and Danielle Azzariti, training for biocurators has not yet begun, but, as of 7/13/22 there is a plan in place for training.)

Responses to Feedback provided on Sept 9, 2022 by the ClinGen Group & Personnel Management System:

Among the 233 genes on our list, there are a total of 45 that do not appear on other GCEP lists (188 do appear on other lists, not 94 as stated above - apologies for the error!). As of 9/30/22, the not-curated include the following: ACER3, AIFM2, AIMP2, AMPD2, APP, CLDN11, COX7B, CST3, DEGS1, EIF2AK1, EIF2AK2, ELOVL1, ENTPD1, HIKESHI, ISCA2, ITM2B, LSM11, LSM7, LSS, MAL, MTTP, NKX6-2, NOTCH2NLA, PI4KA, PLD3, PLEKHG2, POLR3K, RNF220, RNU7-1, RPIA, SAMD9L, SLC13A3, SLC35B2, STAT1, STN1, TM4SF20, TMEM106B, TMEM163, TMEM63A, TREM2, TRMT10A, TYROBP, UFM1, VPS11, VPS18 This list of 45 genes will be our top priority for gene curation.

The top priority genes to work with other groups include: GFAP, PLP1, TUBB4A, RNASEH2A/B/C, TREX1, SAMHD1, ADAR1, IFIH1, EIF2B1-5, POLR3A/B, SNORD118.

Also, per the feedback, we have added additional experts to this GCEP. See above. The following genes have been curated as ‘Definitive’ by the ClinGen Parkinson’s Disease GCEP: GBA1, SNCA, VPS35, PARK7, PRKN, LRRK2, and PINK1.

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:29","data":{"uuid":"3d82e9ed-250f-40b3-ac23-e8fdee9f3663","name":"Alport Syndrome","description":"Alport Syndrome is the most common genetic renal disorder and encompasses a spectrum of phenotypes ranging from isolated, non-progressive hematuria through to progressive nephropathy characterized by hematuria, proteinuria and chronic kidney disease, and end-stage renal disease. Affected individuals may also have progressive sensorineural hearing loss and ocular abnormalities.

The Alport Syndrome Variant Curation Expert Panel (VCEP) will focus on the 3 genes implicated in this disorder: COL4A5, causative of X-linked Alport Syndrome, and COL4A3 and COL4A4, causative of autosomal dominant Alport Syndrome and autosomal recessive Alport Syndrome.

The Alport Syndrome VCEP will establish specifications to the existing ACMG/AMP variant classification guidelines for Alport Syndrome genes and will provide expert curation of clinically relevant variants according to these modified through to progressive nephropathy characterized by hematuria, proteinuria, and chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease. Affected individuals may also have progressive sensorineural hearing loss and ocular abnormalities.

The Alport Syndrome Variant Curation Expert Panel (VCEP) will focus on the 3 genes implicated in this disorder: COL4A5, causative of X-linked Alport Syndrome , and COL4A3 and COL4A4, causative of autosomal dominant Alport Syndrome and autosomal recessive Alport Syndrome.

The Alport Syndrome VCEP will establish specifications to the existing ACMG/AMP variant classification guidelines for Alport Syndrome genes and will provide expert curation of clinically relevant variants according to these modified rules.","membership_description":"Our membership has a diverse scope of expertise. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:21","data":{"uuid":"dd9239ae-fc2f-45fa-bb3b-7c4a2a2a6fcb","name":"Complement-Mediated Kidney Diseases","description":"The broad scope of the Complement-Mediated Kidney Diseases Gene Curation Expert Panel (GCEP) will be to curate genes associated with complement-mediated kidney diseases, with an initial focus on thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA), particularly atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome (aHUS), and C3 glomerulopathy (C3G). Genetic forms of TMA that do not implicate the complement system (such as those due to defects in coagulation pathway genes) will also be an area of focus. To date, defects in ~40 genes have been asserted to cause these diseases and this list will continue to expand. The Complement-Mediated Kidney Diseases GCEP will first curate complement-mediated disorders that result in a primarily renal phenotype. Genes with a primary phenotype outside the scope of our work will be left until the end of phase 1 curations, with the expectation that they will only be curated if no other GCEP plans to curate them. The Complement-Mediated Kidney Diseases GCEP will also work closely with the Immunology CDWG, with representatives included in the GCEP membership. Genes of interest that overlap with existing GCEPs (e.g. Hemostasis/Thrombosis GCEP or Glomerulopathy GCEPs) will be identified and resolved, and where significant overlap exists with the purview of other GCEPs, curations will be jointly reviewed. Kidney Diseases","short_name":"Kidney Immune","scope_description":"The broad scope of the Complement-Mediated Kidney Diseases Gene Curation Expert Panel (GCEP) will be to curate genes associated with complement-mediated kidney diseases, with an initial focus on thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA), particularly atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome (aHUS), and C3 glomerulopathy (C3G). Genetic forms of TMA that do not implicate the complement system (such as those due to defects in coagulation pathway genes) will also be an area of focus. To date, defects in ~40 genes have been asserted to cause these diseases and this list will continue to expand. The Complement-Mediated Kidney Diseases GCEP will first curate complement-mediated disorders that result in a primarily renal phenotype. Genes with a primary phenotype outside the scope of our work will be left until the end of phase 1 curations, with the expectation that they will only be curated if no other GCEP plans to curate them. The Complement-Mediated Kidney Diseases GCEP will also work closely with the Immunology CDWG, with representatives included in the GCEP membership. Genes of interest that overlap with existing GCEPs (e.g. Hemostasis/Thrombosis GCEP or Glomerulopathy GCEPs) will be identified and resolved, and where significant overlap exists with the purview of other GCEPs, curations will be jointly reviewed. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:20","data":{"uuid":"b708f67f-bf5f-4237-ac90-da64ae5576bb","name":"BCR::ABL1-like B-lymphoblastic Leukemia/Lymphoma SC-","description":"The World Health Organization (WHO) recently designated BCR::ABL1-like B-ALL as an entity within the 5th edition  
of the Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours and acknowledged its genetic heterogeneity (PMID: 35732829).  
Moreover, the Internal Consensus Committee (ICC) has divided BCR::ABL1-like B-ALL into three subgroups: ABL-  
class rearranged, JAK/STAT activated, and not otherwise specified (PMID: 35767897). In keeping with the  
recommendations of the CDWG Oversight Committee, the SC-VCEP will therefore evaluate genetic alterations  
described in each of these disease subgroups and recognized in the medical literature, as well as by the WHO and  
ICC, as being implicated in pathogenesis of BCR::ABL1-like B-ALL. Specifically, ABL-class gene rearrangements of  
ABL1, ABL2, CSF1R, PDGFRA, and PDGFRB shall be examined, as well as rearrangements of CRLF2, JAK2,  
EPOR, TSLP, and IL2RB, and point mutations of JAK2, IL7R, SH2B3, JAK1, JAK3, TSLP, and IL2RB, which are  
implicated in JAK/STAT signaling. Additional rearrangements of DGKH, PTK2B, LYN, TYK2, and BLNK will also be  
considered, but those involving FLT3, NTRK3, and FGFR1 shall be reserved for the discussions of SC-VCEPs

on first. Refer to Section 2.1 of the VCEP Protocol for guidance and the established VCEP webpages on clinicalgenome.org for examples.\n\nThe NF VCEP is composed of 5 subcommittees dealing with Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) and 2 (NF2), Legius Syndrome (SPRED1) and Schwannomatosis (SMARCB1 and LZTR1). Given the broad overlap of the phenotypes and the expertise available in these groups, a single VCEP covering these disease phenotypes is proposed. The expert panel subcommittees are defined in the Member list and Figure 1. The gene-specific ACMG/AMP classification criteria will be established subsequently. The subcommittees will proceed with NF1, then NF2, then SPRED1, and SMARCB1 & LZTR1 in a staggered process.\n\nFIGURE 1: NF VCEP subcommittees\n\nNeurofibromatosis type 1, type 2 (NF1, NF2), and non‐NF2 schwannomatosis (SCHW), are a group of autosomal dominant genetic disorders that predispose affected individuals to the development of tumors in the nervous system. Variant interpretation and classification on these disease-causative genes are challenging due to the broad mutational spectrum detected in the five genes arising from missense, nonsense, frameshift, in-frame, splice-site, deep intronic, 5’- and 3`-UTR variation, as well as the deletions and other structural rearrangements (e.g., retro-transpositional insertion of Alu elements in NF1) (Messiaen & Wimmer, 2012; Valero et al, 2011; Perez‐Becerril et al, 2021). In general, there are no clear mutational “hotspots.” The interpretation of missense variants located outside the well-known functional domains and the high proportion of non-coding and splicing variants (at least 20% for NF1) is very difficult and may require additional functional assays to be classified. In addition, 50% or more of NF and SCHW-affected patients have de novo variants, most of them showing inconclusive phenotypes at the early stages of the disease, for whom an accurate genetic test is critical for clinical use and follow up.\n\nNF1 subcommitte:\n\nNF1, caused by pathogenic variants (PVs) in the NF1 gene, is characterized by multiple café-au- lait macules (CALMs), skinfold freckling, tumors of the nervous system, an increased risk of certain cancers and other features (Uusitalo et al. 2016; Gutmann et al. 2017). Some of these clinical features are also shown by Legius Syndrome (LGSS) and Constitutional Mismatch Repair Deficiency (CMMRD) patients (Legius el al 2021; Suerink et al 2019), while some NF1 patients could present some Noonan-like features (De Luca et al, 2005). To date, clinically significant genotype-phenotype correlations have been noted for the NF1 microdeletion (reviewed by Kehrer-Sawatzki et al. 2017), as well as for the recurrent in-frame and missense variants (Upadhyaya et al. 2007; Pinna et al. 2015; Rojnueangnit et al. 2015; Koczkowska et al. 2018; Koczkowska et al. 2020).\n\nNF1 is a large and complex gene that spans approximately 350 kb and includes 61 exons, with five alternatively spliced exons. Neurofibromin interacts with SPRED1 to inhibit the RAS-MAPK signaling pathway, which could explain the clinical overlap between NF1 and LGSS and with other RASopathies such as Noonan syndrome. In addition, more than 13 interacting proteins and 7 functional domains has been described. These factors, alongside other evidence including co-segregation, calibrated in silico predictions and functional assays are combined in ACMG-AMP classification model.\n\nNF2 subcommitte:\n\nNF2 is caused by PVs in the NF2 gene. The most characteristic feature of NF2 is the development of bilateral vestibular schwannomas (BVS), in addition to multiple schwannomas in other cranial, peripheral and spinal nerves meningiomas, and ependymomas, among others (Lloyd & Evans 2013). The clinical expression of the disease is highly variable but a strong genotype-phenotype correlation has been detected (Haliday et al, 2017; Catasús et al 2021). More than 50% of de novo NF2 patients are mosaic, making their diagnosis difficult, due to the clinical overlap with SCHW (Smith et al, 2017; Kehrer‑Sawatzki et al, 2018), which is characterized by the development of multiple schwannomas and meningiomas.\n\nNF2 gene codes for merlin (moesin‐ezrin‐radixin‐like protein) and two main isoforms have been identified, each one with 590 and 595 amino acids respectively. Merlin is composed of three structural regions: a N-terminal FERM domain divided in three subdomains (A, B and C), an α-helical coiled-coil domain and a C-terminal hydrophilic domain (CTD). Differences in merlin regarding ERM proteins reside in the lack of the canonical C-terminal actin binding site, fundamental for their activity at the cortical cytoskeleton. In contrast, merlin can interact with actin fibers through residues 1-27 and 280-232. In addition, merlin has a 7-residue conserved blue box motif in which alterations in Drosophila have result in mutants with a dominant-negative activity (Johnson KC, Kissil JL, Fry JL, et al. 2002; Lajeunesse DR, Mccartney BM, Fehon RG. 1998; McClatchey AI, Fehon RG. 2009). Furthermore, no functional assays have been developed to interpret NF2 missense and in-frame variants. Currently, only co-segregation studies, calibrated in silico predictions and tumor analyisis are combined to classify NF2 variants following ACMG-AMP classification model.\n\nSchwannomatosis subcommitte:\n\nSCHW is partially explained by germline PVs in the SMARCB1 and LZTR1 genes. The type and location of germline PVs in the SMARCB1 gene have a marked effect on phenotype: complete loss of function (LoF) of SMARCB1 causes atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (ATRT), while hypomorphic variants represent most of the SCHW–causing variants, many of them located at 5’- and 3’-UTR ends of the gene. Moreover, certain SMARCB1 variants cause Coffin-Siris syndrome (Smith et al., 2014; Kalimuthu & Chetty 2016; Vitte et al., 2017). In addition, LZTR1 PVs associated with SCHW have been identified across the full length of the gene with incomplete penetrance in several cases, and to increase the complexity of this gene, LZTR1 has been described as Noonan-disease causing gene inherited both in autosomal dominant and recessive manner (Johnston et al 2017; Motta et al 2019) and LZTR1 LoF variants are far too common in population to account for the frequency of LZTR1-SCHW. For non-NF2 associated schwannomas, a four-hit, three step model has been described, consisting in a germline SMARCB1 or LZTR1 pathogenic variant, followed by the total or partial loss of chromosome 22 (involving SMARCB1 or LZTR1 and NF2 loci), and finally, a somatic mutation in the remaining wild type allele of NF2 (Sestini R, Bacci C, Provenzano A, et al, 2008).\n\nThe Expert Panel for SMARCB1 and LZTR1 genes will start with the interpretation, classification, and curation of constitutional SMARCB1 and LZTR1 variants that are associated with non-NF2 Schwannomatosis, including the development of gene-specific ACMG/AMP criteria for variant classification. Thus, this VCEP subcommittee will provide assessment regarding the pathogenicity of variants in SMARCB1 and LZTR1 with respect to an Schwannomatosis phenotype.\n\nLegius subcommitte:\n\nLegius syndrome is characterized by multiple café au lait macules without neurofibromas or other tumor manifestations related to NF1. Additional clinical manifestations reported commonly include intertriginous freckling, lipomas, macrocephaly, and learning disabilities / ADHD / developmental delays. Legius syndrome is caused by PV in SPRED1 gene, which is shorter than NF1, spanning 104.4 kb of genomic sequence that codes for a transcript of 7 kb in length with an open reading frame coding for 444 amino acids. At functional level, it is known its interaction to NF1 and its contribution to RAS-MAPK pathway negative regulation.\n\nDue to the low number of patients described and its clinical overlap with NF1, an internationally coordinated effort is needed for variant curation in this gene with a multifactorial approach combining family segregation studies, molecular characteristics, in silico and protein structural analysis, and functional assays.","membership_description":"The proposed expert panel includes two co-PIs – one US-based (Dr. Scott Plotkin) and one from Europe (Dr. Elisabeth Castellanos) in addition to 33 diverse leading experts from the contents of Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2), Legius Syndrome (LGSS) and Schwannomatosis (SCHW), or other related hereditary pathologies, mostly hereditary disease with tumor predisposition (hereditary cancer). In addition, this VCEP has many key members (20) that are highly experienced in ACMG application and guidelines, as well as 5 members are actively engaged in at least one other ClinGen expert panel or working group (see “Area and Type of Expertise\" for further details). Many of them combine their clinical care work with NF/SCHW-related research. Furthermore, high-volume diagnostic labs (public and private) have been invited to participate in this VCEP.\n\nThis panel of experts includes clinical and molecular geneticists, variant scientists, genetic counselors, epidemiology, neurosurgery, etc ...) who regularly participate in the diagnosis and/or clinical management of this group of pathologies. Symptoms, including hypotonia and movement disorders (especially oculogyric crisis and dystonia) as well as autonomic dysfunction and behavioral disorders, vary extensively and typically emerge in the first months of life. However, diagnosis is difficult, requiring analysis of metabolites in cerebrospinal fluid, assessment of plasma AADC activity, and/or DNA sequence analysis, and is frequently delayed for years. New metabolomics techniques promise early diagnosis of AADC deficiency by detection of 3-O-methyl-dopa in serum or dried blood spots.

In pursuit of providing high quality and systematic curation of DDC variants, we have assembled a panel of clinical, academic, and industry experts in the fields of genetics, pediatric neurology, functional genetics, and biochemistry. With research progressing and gene therapy emerging, determining the pathogenicity of variants is critical to ongoing efforts towards diagnosis and patient care. Decarboxylase (Aromatic L-Amino Acid Decarboxylase)","short_name":"DDC","scope_description":"Aromatic-L-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC) deficiency is an ultra-rare inherited autosomal recessive disorder characterized by sharply reduced synthesis of dopamine as well as other neurotransmitters. Symptoms, including hypotonia and movement disorders (especially oculogyric crisis and dystonia) as well as autonomic dysfunction and behavioral disorders, vary extensively and typically emerge in the first months of life. However, diagnosis is difficult, requiring analysis of metabolites in cerebrospinal fluid, assessment of plasma AADC activity, and/or DNA sequence analysis, and is frequently delayed for years. New metabolomics techniques promise early diagnosis of AADC deficiency by detection of 3-O-methyl-dopa in serum or dried blood spots.

Human AADC is encoded by the DDC gene, which is present in a single copy located on the short arm of chromosome 7 (band 7p12.1-p12.3). The full gene spans >107 kb and comprises 15 exons (ranging from 20 to 400 bp in length) separated by introns ranging from 1.0 to 17.0 kb in length. The non-neuronal and neuronal DDC mRNAs differed only in the region corresponding to the untranslated first exon. The first exon for the non-neuronal-type mRNA was located 4.2 kilobases upstream to that for the neuronal-type mRNA and 22 kilobases from exon 2, to which it is spliced. Thus, an alternative usage of the first exon in the 5′-untranslated regions produces 2 types of mRNAs in DDC and suggested that alternative splicing would regulate the tissue-specific expression of AADC.

As the range of variants leading to AADC deficiency continues to expand with the identification of new patients, several specific variants should be noted. In a study of 37 ethnically Chinese patients, 76% of all DDC variants were c.714+4A>T. Biochemical and cell biology analyses revealed that the most frequent missense variant, the p.S250F variant, results in catalytic and folding defects. It was also proposed that pyridoxine may act as a pharmacologic chaperone on the holo form of this variant.

Several public databases (ClinVar, Pediatric Neurotransmitter Diseases database PNDdb, Human Gene Mutation Database, HMDB, and Leiden Open Variation Database, LOVD) list more than 270 different DDC variant. Unfortunately, many variants are still listed as VUS.

In pursuit of providing high quality and systematic curation of DDC variants, we have assembled a panel of clinical, academic, and industry experts in the fields of genetics, pediatric neurology, functional genetics, and biochemistry. With research progressing and gene therapy emerging, determining the pathogenicity of variants is critical to ongoing efforts towards diagnosis and patient care. Curation efforts will synergize with ongoing functional studies, analyses of function/ genotype/phenotype correlations, and efforts to identify effective precision medications.","membership_description":"Dr. Nenad Blau has several publications with pediatric neurotransmitter genes, including DDC (PMID: 33903016, 32668217, 30952622, 16917893). He is curator of PAH, DDC, PTS, GCH1, QDPR, SPR and PCBD1 locus-specific databases at http://www.biopku.org and founder and chair of the IEMbase the knowledgebase of inherited metabolic diseases at http://www.iembase.org. Dr. Blau also established the BIOPKU database with more than 17,000 PAH genotypes and phenotypes.

All experts panel members have experience with DDC variants assessment and diagnosis of AADC deficiency, including numerous publications.

Of the VCEP members, Dr. Sarah Elsea, Dr. Nastassja Himmelreich and Dr. Carla Carducci regularly use ACMG/AMP guidelines to classify variants and/or review variants during clinical laboratory case sign-out.","type":"vcep","date_completed":null,"inactive_date":null,"current_step":3,"all_genes":[{"hgnc_id":2719,"gene_symbol":"DDC","mondo_id":"MONDO:0012084","disease_name":"aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase deficiency","disease_entity":null}],"clinvar_org_id":null,"vcep_definition_approval":"2022-05-10T04:00:00.000000Z","vcep_draft_specification_approval":"2025-01-23T20:07:26.000000Z","vcep_pilot_approval":null,"vcep_final_approval":null},"parent":{"uuid":"2e312275-a400-47ab-a34c-cad7698703cf","name":"Inborn Errors Metabolism","type":"cdwg","status":"active"}}}

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:18","data":{"uuid":"229bd167-6f25-44f3-8291-c21d7da4f165","name":"Limb Girdle Muscular Dystrophy","description":"The goal of this panel is to resolve the diagnosis of limb girdle muscular dystrophy (LGMD) by establishing an algorithm to genetically define clinically affected individuals without a confirmed diagnosis. LGMDs are a group of genetically heterogenous disorders sharing a common phenotype of proximal limb girdle pattern muscle weakness and wasting. They are chronically progressive, with no FDA approved therapies, and cause significant lifetime morbidity. Advances in technologies targeting gene replacement have led to a number of candidate drugs in the pipeline creating a pressing need to genetically diagnose patients. Indeed, a phase I/II gene therapy trial has delivered β-sarcoglycan to three LGMDR4 patients. The advent of broad-based free genetic testing programs in the United States offered by the MDA, and the Jain Foundation have increased the diagnosis and prevalence of each LGMD subtype. However, this approach has also created a new diagnostic challenge for clinically affected patients: with approximately 55% of patients having variants of uncertain significance (VUS) in an LGMD gene, and 23% with no mutation identified despite genetic testing. This VCEP will classify variants in established LGMD genes using the American College of Medical Genetics (ACMG) Guidelines. Our genes of interest for initial specification have either a strong or definitive gene curation classification, they are as follows: *SGCA, SGCB, SGCD, SGCG, CAPN3, DYSF, ANO5, Girdle Muscular Dystrophy","short_name":"LGMD","scope_description":"The goal of this panel is to resolve the diagnosis of limb girdle muscular dystrophy (LGMD) by establishing an algorithm to genetically define clinically affected individuals without a confirmed diagnosis. LGMDs are a group of genetically heterogenous disorders sharing a common phenotype of proximal limb girdle pattern muscle weakness and wasting. They are chronically progressive, with no FDA approved therapies, and cause significant lifetime morbidity. Advances in technologies targeting gene replacement have led to a number of candidate drugs in the pipeline creating a pressing need to genetically diagnose patients. Indeed, a phase I/II gene therapy trial has delivered β-sarcoglycan to three LGMDR4 patients. The advent of broad-based free genetic testing programs in the United States offered by the MDA, and the Jain Foundation have increased the diagnosis and prevalence of each LGMD subtype. However, this approach has also created a new diagnostic challenge for clinically affected patients: with approximately 55% of patients having variants of uncertain significance (VUS) in an LGMD gene, and 23% with no mutation identified despite genetic testing. This VCEP will classify variants in established LGMD genes using the American College of Medical Genetics (ACMG) Guidelines. Our genes of interest for initial specification have either a strong or definitive gene curation classification, they are as follows: SGCA, SGCB, SGCD, SGCG, CAPN3, DYSF, ANO5, FKRP.","membership_description":null,"type":"vcep","date_completed":"2024-12-23T05:00:00.000000Z","inactive_date":null,"current_step":4,"all_genes":[{"hgnc_id":3097,"gene_symbol":"DYSF","mondo_id":"MONDO:0015152","disease_name":"autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":10809,"gene_symbol":"SGCG","mondo_id":"MONDO:0015152","disease_name":"autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":10806,"gene_symbol":"SGCB","mondo_id":"MONDO:0015152","disease_name":"autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":10807,"gene_symbol":"SGCD","mondo_id":"MONDO:0015152","disease_name":"autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":1480,"gene_symbol":"CAPN3","mondo_id":"MONDO:0015152","disease_name":"autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":27337,"gene_symbol":"ANO5","mondo_id":"MONDO:0015152","disease_name":"autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":10805,"gene_symbol":"SGCA","mondo_id":"MONDO:0015152","disease_name":"autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy","disease_entity":null},{"hgnc_id":17997,"gene_symbol":"FKRP","mondo_id":"MONDO:0700066","disease_name":"myopathy caused by variation in FKRP","disease_entity":null}],"clinvar_org_id":"509833","vcep_definition_approval":"2020-03-10T04:00:00.000000Z","vcep_draft_specification_approval":"2022-06-09T04:00:00.000000Z","vcep_pilot_approval":"2024-12-12T05:00:00.000000Z","vcep_final_approval":"2024-12-23T05:00:00.000000Z"},"parent":{"uuid":"422c495c-a77d-4a70-aa60-2bee7d0ca980","name":"Neurological Disorders","type":"cdwg","status":"active"}}}

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:17","data":{"uuid":"a56f9abc-d921-42bb-87c6-c0b98e7449bf","name":"Kidney Cystic and Ciliopathy Disorders","description":"Kidney Cystic and Ciliopathy Disorders VCEP will focus on cystic kidney disease, with an initial focus on Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD). This group plans to begin with the most common and highly penetrant genes within our area of focus for variant curation and steadily enlarge the scope of the project overtime to encompass a wider range of cystic kidney disease genes, as outlined below.

The Kidney Cystic and Ciliopathy Disorders VCEP aims to specify the existing ACMG guidelines for variant classification in PKD genes and review variants for expert classification including resolving discrepancies in existing variant classification in ClinVar prioritizing the most common genes and variants associated to PKD.

**Phase 1: Focus on *PKD1***

We expect that initial efforts will focus on the key player in autosomal dominant PKD (*PKD1*) which poses considerable issues surrounding the implementation of the ACMG/AMP guidelines and is responsible for approximately 78% of PKD cases. The establishment of guidelines surrounding pathogenicity assessment of previously reported plus unreported missense variants in *PKD1* has been identified as a key goal of the VCEP.

**Phase 2: Widen scope to include all autosomal dominant PKD genes**

Once guidelines for *PKD1* have been established, the expert panel’s expertise will be applied to the wider range of autosomal dominant PKD genes (*PKD2, GANAB, ALG9* and *DNAJB11*) as well as the autosomal recessive PKD (ARPKD) gene, {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:17","data":{"uuid":"259c3053-f41a-4b14-922e-3e526df6c6d2","name":"NTRK Fusions Somatic Cancer","description":"Neurotrophic receptor tyrosine kinase (NTRK) fusions are well-recognized oncogenic drivers in various pediatric and adult malignancies (PMID: [30333516, 31075511](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/30333516,31075511)). Small-molecule kinase inhibitors targeting constitutive signaling by oncogenic NTRK fusions have been approved by the FDA for tumors harboring NTRK fusions (PMID: [30901551](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/30901551)).\n\nThe NTRK expert panel will develop a sustainable process for systematic consensus review of fusions and associated secondary alterations in *NTRK1, NTRK2,* and *NTRK3* genes that drive oncogenesis and response to targeted therapy. The group will focus on further specifying and piloting the AMP/ASCO/CAP guidelines (PMID: [27993330](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27993330)) for somatic variant classification and the diagnostic and therapeutic significance of NTRK fusions in cancers. After consensus classification, the panel will make these interpretations publicly available through the CIViC {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:14","data":{"uuid":"04eddacd-ac35-43f6-b0dc-c76b6d75e247","name":"Hemoglobinopathy","description":"The Hemoglobinopathy VCEP is a joint effort of the [ITHANET](http://www.ithanet.eu) portal for hemoglobinopathies and the [Global Globin Network by the Human Variome Project](http://www.humanvariomeproject.org/gg2020/index.html). This VCEP will focus on the review and annotation of variants that are related to hemoglobinopathies, the most common monogenic diseases in the world. They are caused by variants in the globin gene clusters, namely α-globin locus (NG\_000006) which includes genes *HBA1*, *HBA2* and *HBZ* and β-globin locus (NG\_000007) which includes genes *HBB*, *HBD*, *HBG1*, *HBG2* and *HBE* and the regulatory element LCRB. Although different disease/phenotype ontologies, such as HPO, Orphanet and ICD, provide different classifications, hemoglobinopathies can be broadly divided into the following conditions:

1. ***Thalassaemias***, which can be further divided into the following conditions: 
    1. α-thalassaemia
    2. β-thalassaemia
    3. δ-thalassaemia
    4. δβ-thalassaemia
    5. εγδβ-thalassaemia and γδβ-thalassaemia
2. ***Sickle-cell disorders***
3. ***Other hemoglobinopathies***, which include Hemoglobin E disease, Hemoglobin C disease and Hemoglobin D disease, amongst other rarer structural hemoglobinopathies and hemoglobinopathies that result from compound heterozygosity of structural variants with thalassaemia or sickle-cell disease
4. ***Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin*** (HPFH)

The VCEP will begin the variant annotation with the genes located in the globin gene clusters, which carry the most common and highly penetrant variants. Full lists of genes and variants are available on the ITHANET portal, as follows:

1. 1. Genes: <http://www.ithanet.eu/db/ithagenes?action=glist>
    2. Variants: <http://www.ithanet.eu/db/ithagenes?action=list>

The VCEP will prioritize the review of variants that are currently submitted in ClinVar, particularly those with conflicting interpretations. In addition and to enable broader benefit to the community, the Panel will make an early submission of all variants stored in the IthaGenes database, before completing the review of all the After interpreting variants using these specified guidelines, the group will make their final interpretations publicly available through ClinVar.\n\nThis VCEP plans to begin ACMG/AMP rule specification for genes associated with Glanzmann thrombasthenia (OMIM: 273800). These genes include *ITGA2B* and *ITGB3*. The group then plans to move to rule specifications for Bernard-Soulier syndrome (OMIM: 231200), including the *GP9*, *GP1BA* and *GP1BB* genes. These two platelet disorders are well studied and their biology is well understood, and these genes are commonly ordered for testing in hematology practices. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:13","data":{"uuid":"24fb3b70-5af4-4cab-b66b-7480eda33a3a","name":"Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor Mutations (SNVs)","description":"Fibroblast growth factor receptors (FGFRs) are highly conserved, widely distributed transmembrane tyrosine kinase receptors that are involved in development, differentiation, cell survival, migration, angiogenesis, and carcinogenesis \[PMID: 23696246, 10966463\]. Deregulation of FGFR signaling, via gene amplification, overexpression, point mutations or chromosomal translocations has been implicated in several human cancers, such as bladder, lung, breast, prostate, and endometrial cancers. Since the identification of FGFR as a relevant target for cancer therapy, a number of small-molecular kinase inhibitors targeting constitutive signaling by oncogenic FGFR genetic alterations have been approved by the FDA for tumors harboring these lesions \[PMID: [31803255](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31803255/)\].

This FGFR VCEP, formed under the leadership of the Somatic Genitourinary Task Force, is the first variant curation expert panel forming within this somatic cancer subgroup. In the last few years, a handful of FGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), such as erdafitinib and infigratinib, have been approved for the treatment of FGFR2/3 mutated bladder cancer, as well as for other cancers such as cholangiocarcinomas. This promising expectation for targeted therapy has opened the path to routine large-scale sequencing, which has in turn unveiled a large number of variants that we are not able to clearly define and classify due to their rarity. Thus, there is a need to create standard operating procedures that will define application of the AMP/ASCO/CAP guidelines for interpretation of FGFR variants arising from sequencing assays \[PMID: [27993330](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27993330/)\].

The FGFR VCEP will develop a sustainable process for systematic consensus review of mutations in FGFR that drive oncogenesis and response to targeted therapy. To this end, it will work on establishing specifications to the AMP/ASCO/CAP guidelines for FGFR, interpret variants according to these rules, draw upon the expertise of other existing clinical lab practice standards for evaluating variants, and publish the interpretations through the Clinical Interpretations of Variants in Cancer (CIViC) platform and ClinVar database. While the initial focus of this EP has been on FGFR3 in bladder cancer, this VCEP will expand the scope of its guideline specifications to the annotation of additional FGFR genes (namely FGFR1, FGFR2 and FGFR4), and include rearrangements as well as SNVs in other cancers. The aim would be to apply the newly created and approved rules to generate expert-panel approved variant submissions.

The proposed scope of the work is further delineated below:

Tumor types:

1\. Urothelial tumours which have a high incidence (>20%) of FGFR mutations \[PMID: [26373574](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26373574/)\]

2\. Tumors with lower incidence (1~5%) of FGFR mutations: multiple tumor types \[PMID: {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:10","data":{"uuid":"bdfc67d3-58fc-460c-8f43-c4dc72b2d24c","name":"Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Disease","description":"Severe Combined Immune Deficiency (SCID) represents one of the most severe inborn errors of immunity (IEI). The term “combined” indicates that patients with these disorders have defects in both T cell and B cell function, making them susceptible to life-threatening viral, fungal, and bacterial infections.

The IUIS IEI Committee was originally created under the auspices of the World Health Organization in 1973 to describe and classify the types of primary immune defects (PIDDs) affecting humans. The committee is tasked with maintaining a list of immunologic disorders. Since the 1970s, it has maintained a curated list to ensure a standard nomenclature and consistent approach. After discovery of the first genetic defects associated with PIDD, the committee has also maintained a list of genes that have been associated with IEI in peer-reviewed published reports. This list is updated approximately every 3 years and published online and in print. The most recent update was performed in 2019 and published in January 2020. It lists 430 genes that have been associated with IEI in peer-reviewed publications. These 430 genes are divided across 10 tables based on their associations with various clinical immunodeficiency phenotypes.

This VCEP will focus on the genes associated with a SCID phenotype included in Table 1 of the IUIS document, which encompasses 18 genes: *IL2RG, JAK3, IL7R, PTPRC, CD3D, CD3E, CD247, CORO1A, LAT, RAG1, RAG2, DCLRE1C, PRKDC, NHEJ1, LIG4, ADA, AK2,* and *RAC2,*  as well as  *FOXN1*. Variants in *RMRP* will also be curated because *RMRP* deficiency stands as the 6<sup>th</sup> most common molecular cause of abnormal newborn screening tests for SCID in the United States. The SCID VCEP will first work on specifying the ACMG variant curation guidelines for 8 of these 18 genes, including: *IL2RG, RAG1, IL7R, ADA, JAK3, DCLRE1C, RAG2*, *RMRP*  and {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:10","data":{"uuid":"9fb2eb90-8b4b-4504-b83e-08eaf10b108e","name":"Congenital Myopathies","description":"Hereditary primary muscle diseases include a range of disorders that primarily affect the skeletal muscle and result from defects in genes related to muscle structure and function. Major groups of hereditary primary muscle diseases include congenital myopathies and muscular dystrophies. Congenital myopathies are a group of neuromuscular disorders that typically present at birth with hypotonia and muscle weakness, with an estimated prevalence of ~1-5/100,000 live births. As opposed to muscular dystrophies that are characterized by progressive muscle degeneration and wasting, congenital myopathies tend to be non-progressive or slowly progressive and do not involve continuous degeneration and regeneration in the muscle. Patients often have respiratory insufficiency and feeding difficulties, and cardiac involvement may also be observed. Congenital myopathies are defined by characteristic structural changes in the skeletal muscle, which also forms a basis for their sub-classification, such as nemaline myopathy, centronuclear myopathy, central core disease, multiminicore disease, and congenital fiber type disproportion. To date, over 30 genes have been associated with various forms of congenital myopathies and our Congenital Myopathies Gene Curation Expert Panel has curated 20 genes regarding the level of evidence for their disease association so far. Sixteen genes (*DNM2, KLHL40, LMOD3, ACTA1, MTM1, NEB, CFL2, PYROXD1, STAC3, TNNT1, MYPN, SEPN1/SELENON, MYH2, MEGF10, SPEG, RYR1*) were identified as having Definitive evidence to have a role in congenital myopathies. Each of these genes have several hundred variants reported in patients with often limited amount of data in the literature for their association with disease, which poses a challenge in the clinical setting. The Congenital Myopathies VCEP aims to (1) define criteria for the specific classification of variants related to congenital myopathies based on the ACMG standards and guidelines for variant interpretation and classification, and (2) review the majority of reported variants that have been associated with these disorders to provide expert-approved classification for their clinical significance, starting with 5 genes that have been described as having Definitive evidence for a causal role in disease:* ACTA1, NEB, MTM1, DNM2, {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:07","data":{"uuid":"a5b815b7-1a4f-423c-87de-42081823262f","name":"Antibody Deficiencies","description":"The focus of the Antibody Deficiencies Variant Curation Expert Panel will be to curate variants in genes associated with various inborn errors of immunity that result in antibody deficiencies (decrease in one or more immunoglobulin isotypes) with or without quantitative B cell defects and other phenotypic manifestations. The VCEP will consider genes found to be associated with antibody immunodeficiencies, as outlined by the International Union of Immunological Societies (IUIS) Expert Committee 2019 publication: ([PMID: 31953710](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7082301/)). To start, the VCEP will consider the following three genes: *CTLA4*, *PIK3CD*, and *PIK3R1*. Variants in these genes will be curated using the VCEP modified-ACMG criteria. Variants will be identified from ClinVar, various primary immunodeficiency databases, both public and private, and variants will be consolidated by gene, and then categorized, based on literature with functional, immunological and clinical phenotype data. The panel will curate variants based on several lines of evidence. Since many of the inborn errors of immunity genes can have a spectrum of clinical and immunological phenotypes, ranging from complete loss-of-function (LOF), gain-of-function (GOF), partial LOF (hypomorphic or leaky defects), haploinsufficiency and dominant negative, variants will be classified based on current knowledge as well as experimental data. Variants will be curated based on gene priority and relatively more common humoral defects first before tackling the ultra-rare antibody deficiencies.

**Step 2 Approval**:

- August 25, 2022 - VCEP received approval to pilot ACMG/AMP specifications for *CTLA4*
- July 12, 2023 - VCEP received approval to pilot ACMG/AMP specifications for {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:07","data":{"uuid":"2cffd17b-b870-42c4-89b4-e0ac745345a9","name":"Urea Cycle Disorders","description":"The urea cycle disorders (UCDs) comprise a group of genetic disorders impairing the urea cycle, which is responsible for the conversion of ammonia to urea for urinary excretion. In the absence of functional urea cycle, ammonia can accumulate in the body and lead to severe clinical consequences including lethargy, poor feeding, vomiting, and encephalopathy, coma, and death. Other UCDs not associated with acute hyperammonemia may present with clinical phenotypes including neurological or psychiatric symptoms, behavioral problems, lethargy, psychosis, and encephalopathy.

There are eight genes in the urea cycle, and they can be divided into three groups; proximal urea cycle (*CPS1*, *NAGS*, *OTC*), distal urea cycle (*ASS1*, *ASL*, *ARG1*); and transporters (*SLC25A13*, *SLC25A15*). The UCD Expert Panel members have grouped these into Tier 1 (proximal urea cycle), Tier 2 (distal urea cycle), and Tier 3 (transporters) for variant curation purposes.

Tier 1 genes are highest priority for variant curation, as they encompass the earliest onset and most severe disorders of the urea cycle. The proximal UCDs are characterized by severe and often fatal neonatal onset disease with clinical phenotypes including feeding difficulties, neonatal hyperammonemia, lethargy proceeding to coma and death. Individuals with later onset milder disease have also been reported. *OTC* is X-linked. Penetrance in females with pathogenic variants in *OTC* is estimated to be between 20-30% and is influenced by the pattern of X-inactivation in hepatocytes. OTC deficiency is the result of a pathogenic variant in the X-Linked Ornithine transcarbamylase gene \[OTC; MIM#300461\]. OTC Deficiency is the most common UCD, with an estimated worldwide incidence of 1/50,000. Given its severe neonatal presentation and significant morbidity and mortality risks, *OTC* was added to the list of reportable ACMG Secondary Findings v2.0 (Kalia, 2016) and is present on many Carrier Screening Panels, making it a high priority for a concerted effort to deposit well curated variants in ClinVar.

After the Tier 1 genes are complete, we will proceed with Tier 2 and Tier 3 genes The proximal UCDs are characterized by severe and often fatal neonatal onset disease with clinical phenotypes including feeding difficulties, neonatal hyperammonemia, lethargy proceeding to coma and death. Individuals with later onset milder disease have also been reported. OTC is X-linked. Penetrance in females with pathogenic variants in OTC is estimated to be between 20-30% and is influenced by the pattern of X-inactivation in hepatocytes. OTC deficiency is the result of a pathogenic variant in the X-Linked Ornithine transcarbamylase gene \[OTC; MIM#300461\]. OTC Deficiency is the most common UCD, with an estimated worldwide incidence of 1/50,000. Given its severe neonatal presentation and significant morbidity and mortality risks, OTC was added to the list of reportable ACMG Secondary Findings v2.0 (Kalia, 2016) and is present on many Carrier Screening Panels, making it a high priority for a concerted effort to deposit well curated variants in ClinVar.

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:05","data":{"uuid":"c4b02201-94ca-4bc3-930b-edd21f11d687","name":"von Willebrand Disease","description":"von Willebrand Disease (VWD) is classified as a mucocutaneous bleeding disorder that is caused by the deficiency or dysfunction of plasma von Willebrand Factor (VWF). Pathogenic variants in the VWF gene cause VWD.” VWD has six subtypes:

Type 1: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding

Type 2A: Manifests mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding

Type 2B: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding that may be accompanied by thrombocytopenia

Type 2M: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding

Type 2N: Manifests as excessive bleeding, often mimics the bleeding seen in mild to moderate hemophilia A

Type 3: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous, provoked, and musculoskeletal bleeding

The prevalence and inheritance pattern of VWD differs by subtype. Types 1 and 3 are the result of quantitative defects in VWF levels; they are commonly referred to as autosomal dominant and recessive respectively. This is likely due to incomplete penetrance of many of the pathogenic variants leading to quantitative defects; as parents of a child type with type 3 VWD can be, but are not always, diagnosed with type 1 VWD.

Type 2 VWD represents a further group of VWD types where there are qualitative defects in the VWF protein. These types are based on the VWF function that is disrupted. Type 2A, 2B, and 2M are typically inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern with high penetrance of *VWF* pathogenic variants; however, autosomal recessive inheritance has been reported infrequently in type 2A families. Type 2N VWD is inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion, although there is evidence 2N can be semi-dominant. There are relatively good genotype/phenotype correlations for this gene that can also be used to classify patients into the appropriate type.

The von Willebrand Disease Expert Panel will curate clinically relevant variants using the specified classification rules developed by the group. The VWD VCEP would also like to be able to associate *VWF* variants with specific VWD types in ClinVar. This will be an added service to the committee as genotyping is often used by clinicians to inform the type, which can alter clinical care and recurrence risks for family Pathogenic variants in the VWF gene cause VWD.” VWD has six types:

Type 1: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding Type 2A: Manifests mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding Type 2B: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding that may be accompanied by thrombocytopenia Type 2M: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous and provoked bleeding Type 2N: Manifests as excessive bleeding, often mimics the bleeding seen in mild to moderate hemophilia A Type 3: Manifests as mild-to-severe mucocutaneous, provoked, and musculoskeletal bleeding

The prevalence and inheritance pattern of VWD differs by subtype. Types 1 and 3 are the result of quantitative defects in VWF levels; they are commonly referred to as autosomal dominant and recessive respectively. This is likely due to incomplete penetrance of many of the pathogenic variants leading to quantitative defects; as parents of a child type with type 3 VWD can be, but are not always, diagnosed with type 1 VWD.

Type 2 VWD represents a further group of VWD types where there are qualitative defects in the VWF protein. These types are based on the VWF function that is disrupted. Type 2A, 2B, and 2M are typically inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern with high penetrance of VWF pathogenic variants; however, autosomal recessive inheritance has been reported infrequently in type 2A families. Type 2N VWD is inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion, although there is evidence 2N can be semi-dominant. There are relatively good genotype/phenotype correlations for this gene that can also be used to classify patients into the appropriate type.

The von Willebrand Disease Expert Panel will curate clinically relevant variants using the specified classification rules developed by the group. The VWD VCEP would also like to be able to associate VWF variants with specific VWD types in ClinVar. This will be an added service to the committee as genotyping is often used by clinicians to inform the type, which can alter clinical care and recurrence risks for family members. We plan to submit for Step 2 approval for the type 2 specifications (for type 2A, 2B, 2M and 2N), so that we can proceed with a pilot study while continuing to work on rule specifications for types 1 and 3.

Refs: Goodeve, J. and James, P. GeneReviews: von Willebrand disease. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK7014/. Accessed September 26, 2019. Bowman M, et al. The prevalence of symptomatic von Willebrand disease in primary care practice. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:05","data":{"uuid":"61055b29-b655-483b-821a-2266eeaee9d0","name":"X-linked Inherited Retinal Disease","description":"1\. Establish a X-linked Retinal Diseases Expert Panel to address inherited retinal disease genes with an X-linked inheritance pattern (RPGR, CHM, RS1, RP2, OFD1, NDP, and CACNA1F).

2\. Develop Disease and Gene Specific Variant Curation Guidelines. The guidelines for clinical interpretation of genomic sequence variants published American College of Medical Genetics (ACMG) and the Association for Molecular Pathology (AMP) are not disease or gene or variant specific. We will develop appropriate guidelines to use to curate variants in the Inherited Retinal Disease Genes proposed

3\. Apply the Specifications to Curate Variants in Inherited Retinal Disease Genes. To ensure a comprehensive assessment of patient variants we will work with our experts and other diagnostic laboratories to collect and submit to the ClinVar public repository variant and clinical reports held in the private laboratory collections.

4\. Participate in ClinGen working groups. We will participate actively in the Ocular Clinical Domain Working Group, the Inherited Retinal Diseases Gene Working Group, the Sequence Variant Interpretation Working Group, the Biocurator Working Group, the Education, Coordination and Training Working Group, and the monthly ClinGen Scientific webinar.

In summary, at the completion of the proposed work, we will have established variant curation rules that can be applied for all X- linked IRD genes, completed the curation of these IRD genes, and established a framework that can be modified and applied to other genes affecting inherited retinal {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:04","data":{"uuid":"1ca6a3b2-74a7-454e-ac0f-5cda2a1a13bd","name":"Tubulopathy","description":"The broad scope of the Tubulopathy Gene Curation Expert Panel (GCEP) will be to curate genes associated with disorders of water, ion or other transport pathways in any nephron segment from the proximal tubule to the terminal collecting duct. The initial focus will be on Mendelian disorders arising from the altered expression or function of renal tubule-expressed genes, such as those associated with renal tubular acidosis and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. The Tubulopathy GCEP will then go on to address monogenic disorders arising from genes expressed elsewhere that *primarily* impact renal tubular transport function, such as those associated with some forms of familial hypertension or hypophosphatemic rickets. Where significant overlap exists with the purview of other GCEPs, curations will be jointly reviewed. The gene list for curation was selected based on expert consensus, combined with review of literature and of multi-gene sequencing panels from the Genetic Testing Registry, and will be organized in {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:03","data":{"uuid":"007d470f-3321-4193-a790-c05a2020e23b","name":"Syndromic Disorders","description":"**Scope of work for the Syndromic Disorders GCEP:**

The goal of the Syndromic Disorders GCEP is to classify the clinical validity of gene-disease relationships involving multiple body systems and therefore not under the purview of GCEPs that focus on a specific organ system or phenotype.

Sources of gene-disease pairs for curation by the Syndromic Disorders GCEP will include:

- **A prioritized gene list:** Gene-disease pairs currently tested for in clinical laboratories identified through the Genetic Testing Registry (GTR) and prioritized by the number of times the gene is tested for in the GTR and the number of Pathogenic/Likely Pathogenic variants in that gene in ClinVar.
- **A WES/WGS gene list:** Gene-disease pairs identified through clinical whole genome or exome sequencing performed by diagnostic laboratories or research studies with personnel involved in the Syndromic Disorders {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:41:00","data":{"uuid":"a41c9f68-1f40-4e4b-b304-f19c3ed15049","name":"Peroxisomal Disorders","description":"The Peroxisomal Disorders GCEP was formed to examine genes associated with peroxisomal disorders using the ClinGen Clinical Validity Framework. Peroxisomal disorders are grouped into two broad categories: the Peroxisome Biogenesis Disorders (PBD) and single peroxisomal protein/enzyme defects.

The PBD are due to defects in PEX genes, whose encoded PEX proteins (also termed peroxins) are required to assemble functional peroxisomes. Autosomal recessive defects in any one of 13 PEX genes cause the PBD- Zellweger spectrum disorder (ZSD). Defects in PEX7 (and defects in the PEX5 domain that binds to PEX7) cause PBD-Rhizomelic Chondrodysplasia Punctata (RCDP), which has a phenotype distinct from ZSD. Rare dominant alleles have been described for PEX6, PEX14, FAR1 and ACOX1.

Defects in ABCD1, causing X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy, is the most common peroxisome disorder and is considered a single peroxisomal protein defect. The ABCD1 protein is required for the transport of very long chain fatty acids (VLCFA) into the peroxisomal matrix where they can be oxidized. The remaining (rarer) defects feature peroxisomal enzymes/proteins involved in very long chain fatty acid oxidation and plasmalogen biosynthesis. Defects in these genes result in phenotypes that overlap ZSD and RCDP, respectively.

***

This panel focused on the following phenotypes:

* * X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy/AMN and related

  * Zellweger Spectrum Disorder

  * RCDP, ARD and related single enzyme defects

  * Peroxisome single enzyme defects with phenotypes overlapping Zellweger Spectrum Disorder

The primary gene list included:

* * ABCD1, PEX1,2,3,5,6,7,10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 16,19,26,11beta.

  * Related genes: ABCD3, ACBD5, ACOX1, AGPS, AMACR, FAR1, GNPAT, HSD17B4, PHYH, SCP2

  * Genes on Peroxisomal panels reported in GTR: AGXT, BAAT, CAT, DNM1L, DYM, GDAP1, MFF, PEX11alpha, TRIM37

***

**The Peroxisomal Disorders GCEP was formed in 2019, and their initial scope of work included 36 genes thought to be associated with peroxisomal disorders.  The group completed evaluation of these genes in 2023, and their findings are described in PMID:37236006. The General IEM GCEP now re-evaluates these curations on a ClinGen protocol specified periodicity, as described in the **[**ClinGen recuration Diabetes","short_name":"Diabetes","scope_description":"At the commencement of this panel, we chose to focus primarily on the genes known as MODY (maturity onset diabetes of the young) genes, which have etiologies distinct from T1D and T2D, are generally nonsyndromic, appear to account for most cases of monogenic diabetes, and, importantly, include subtypes in which the clinical utility of a molecular diagnosis is well-established. Now we are expanding to a more comprehensive gene-disease validity curation, including other types that could be considered monogenic forms of type 1 and type 2 diabetes. The GCEP will curate 33 genes implicated in monogenic autoimmune and insulin resistance/lipodystrophy forms of diabetes. Additionally, we will curate 31 additional genes implicated in neonatal and later onset monogenic diabetes. Finally, we will curate 16 genes implicated in congenital hyperinsulinism (CHI), 7 of which have already been or will be curated for their relationship with monogenic diabetes but will be split into a different gene-disease validity curation for CHI because of different variants and molecular mechanisms. The genes were selected based on their recommendations for inclusion on the crowd-sourced UK PanelApp monogenic diabetes panels or lack of recommendation on these but inclusion on other U.S. or non-U.S. panels. Genes for autoimmune diabetes and lipodystrophy/insulin resistance will be the subject of the VCEP expansion of gene-specific rules and variant curation, which will be described in a separate VCEP scope of work {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:56","data":{"uuid":"6974482b-bdb6-4abc-92c7-f7cf4a13dab5","name":"Kidney Cystic and Ciliopathy Disorders","description":"The goal of the Kidney Cystic and Ciliopathy Disorders GCEP is to classify the clinical validity of gene-disease relationships involving inherited cystic or tubulointerstitial diseases in the kidney, such as:

1. Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) and autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD).
2. Tubulointerstitial and fibrotic kidney diseases, such as autosomal dominant tubulointerstitial kidney diseases (ADTKD) and nephronophthisis (NPHP).
3. Cystic diseases majorly impacting the liver (autosomal dominant polycystic liver disease; ADPLD).
4. Syndromic disorders due to defects in the functioning of primary cilia (ciliopathies) that have cystic or NPHP kidney phenotypes. Examples include Joubert syndrome (JBTS), Meckel syndrome (MKS), and Bardet Biedl syndrome (BBS).

A list of 121 genes is provided below that was compiled from gene-disease pair lists provided by KidneySeq (Iowa), Gharavi lab (Columbia), PanelApp UK and PanelApp Australia, in addition to consultation with experts in the field.

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:54","data":{"uuid":"ad3c0083-82c8-4d05-9d0c-fa99a00bf615","name":"Hereditary Cancer","description":"**The ClinGen Hereditary Cancer Gene Curation Working Group focuses on curating well-described cancer predisposition genes for their major associated syndromes. In 2014, 114 cancer predisposition genes were identified in Nazneen Rahman's Nature paper which was an early focus of the GCEP work. The HC-GCEP will also establish additional target goals for gene curation in different hereditary cancer fields, and completing recuration of breast, ovarian, colon, and pancreatic cancer genes and polyposis/colon cancer genes. We are utilizing ClinGen’s systematic, evidence-based framework to evaluate the strength of gene-disease relationships between cancer predisposition genes and their major associated syndromes. {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:52","data":{"uuid":"1fa53a54-da44-46f0-a67a-0aa2c8efe8ba","name":"General Inborn Errors of Metabolism","description":"The General Inborn Errors of Metabolism (IEM) GCEP functions as a GCEP for genes asserted to be involved in inborn errors of metabolism that are not currently being curated by any other GCEPs. Of note, the experts in the IEM GCEP have an extensive knowledge of a range of metabolic diseases. However, the experts will consult experts outside the group or invite additional experts to join the group should the need arise.

The General IEM GCEP's objectives are to:

1\) Curate and review genes for IEMs on the RUSP (Recommended Universal Screening Panel for newborns).

2\) Curate and review genes for IEMs that do not currently come under the purview of another GCEP but for which there is a need for the curation to be completed and reviewed.

3\) Curate and review genes for subgroups of IEMs with a small number of gene/disorders (n<~10 curations; e.g. porphyria, disorders of bile acid synthesis, molybdenum metabolism, copper metabolism, etc) for which it would be inefficient to convene a specific GCEP.

Additionally, the General IEM GCEP now recurates for the Lysosomal Diseases, Fatty Acid Oxidation Disorders, Aminoacidopathy, and Peroxisomal Disorders {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:51","data":{"uuid":"3ebfc1a9-a6c2-45d1-8542-c5f8d6587c73","name":"Dilated Cardiomyopathy","description":"The dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) Gene Curation Expert Panel is focused on defining the genes in which pathogenic variants clearly cause dilated cardiomyopathy. Dilated cardiomyopathy is defined as left ventricular enlargement (LVE) accompanied by systolic dysfunction with other usually detectable clinical causes excluded. Systolic dysfunction is most commonly measured by a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF). Echocardiography is the most commonly used method to estimate a LVEF, and a LVEF <50% would be considered systolic dysfunction. While DCM can commonly involve both ventricles (biventricular dysfunction), the left ventricular involvement is necessary to be included into the DCM definition. Thinning of ventricular myocardium commonly occurs with LVE. In contrast, in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), LV wall thickness is greatly increased, and systolic function is preserved or supranormal. Also in contrast, arrhythmogenic right ventricular (RV) cardiomyopathy classically has a very prominent arrhythmic phenotype along with RV involvement, commonly exceeding that of LV involvement. Additional material that provides more detail, and that may be helpful for genetics professionals who are not expert in clinical cardiology is available here: <https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20864896> .\n\nThe DCM gene list includes 116 genes. The list was generated with input from a medical librarian (thorough literature search), OMIM, commercial lab offerings and expert {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:50","data":{"uuid":"d28dc55e-dc14-4974-9f50-e782f49983dd","name":"Craniofacial Malformations","description":"The Craniofacial Malformations Gene Curation Expert Panel will work to define the strength of evidence supporting the associations of genes with various craniofacial malformations. Initial efforts will be focused on evaluating 12 genes in relation to craniosynostoses and skull abnormalities. In later phases of gene curation, we aim to investigate a broader range of disorders including malformations of the ear, midface, jaw, lip, and palate. While many genetic disorders include craniofacial phenotypes, we plan to narrow our scope to genes associated with conditions for which craniofacial malformations are one of the most prominent features, if not the presenting feature. Genes were selected based on expert consensus combined with review of literature and multi-gene sequencing panels from the Genetic Testing Registry (GTR). This process resulted in a list of 145 genes, 12 of which were selected for a first round of curation due to prevalence of variants in patients with craniosynostosis. Round 1 gene list: *EFNB1, ERF, FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3,\\* MEGF8, MSX2, POR, RAB23, SKI, TCF12*, and *TWIST1*. \\*To be done in tandem with the Skeletal Disorders In Round 2 gene curation, we will investigate a broader range of disorders including Frontonasal Dysplasias and Mandibulofacial Dysostoses, while also continuing to curate genes associated with craniosynostosis (SMAD6, SMO, IL11RA, ZIC1, ALX1, ALX3, ALX4, ZSWIM6, POLR1B, POLR1C, POLR1D, SF3B4, DHODH, EFTUD2). While many genetic disorders include craniofacial phenotypes, we plan to narrow our scope to genes associated with conditions for which craniofacial malformations are one of the most prominent features, if not the presenting feature. Genes were selected based on expert consensus combined with review of literature and multi-gene sequencing panels from the Genetic Testing Registry (GTR). {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:48","data":{"uuid":"ed06394d-b912-4f08-a592-67fcc73d72aa","name":"Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease","description":"Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (CMT) is a spectrum of inherited nerve disorders that cause peripheral neuropathy. CMT is regarded as synonymous with “hereditary motor sensory neuropathy” (HMSN). CMT is the most common inherited neurological disorder, affecting approximately 1 in 2500 individuals. Currently, more than 90 genes are reported to cause CMT and are included on clinical genetic testing panels within the Genetic Testing Registry. The goal of the CMT GCEP is to use the ClinGen gene-disease clinical validity framework to evaluate the strength of evidence for these gene-disease associations. These evaluations will enable the development of refined guidelines for clinical diagnosis and management as well as optimize decision making for patients and {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:47","data":{"uuid":"85e38fff-00f3-425b-a123-49dafce05fdd","name":"Brain Malformations","description":"Brain malformations comprise a group of genetic developmental brain disorders that present in childhood with intellectual disability, epilepsy, and other neurologic features, causing substantial morbidity, mortality, and health care costs. We now know that many cases of brain malformation are the result of rare, heritable causes; in other cases, they arise from de novo or somatic mutational events at the gamete or post-zygote stage. In all cases, establishing the correct genomic diagnosis is the cornerstone for proper clinical management.\n\nWe have assembled an Expert Panel with deep, diverse expertise in genetic human brain malformations, including clinical phenotyping, neuroimaging, genetics, neurobiology, and bioinformatics. We have included clinical-researchers with both bench and clinical expertise, including colleagues involved in ClinGen to facilitate integration of our findings into the ClinGen infrastructure. Our group represents innovative genomic research in brain malformation genetics, particularly somatic mutation gene discovery and single-cell genomic approaches to understanding the cell of origin of mutations associated with some {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:47","data":{"uuid":"c64fef51-29a8-45d0-a09a-9f84f63ec003","name":"Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy","description":"The arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) Gene Curation Expert Panel is focused on defining the genes in which pathogenic variants clearly cause arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy. The gene-disease validity curation process includes 6 classification categories describing the level of evidence supporting a given gene-disease relationship: Definitive, Strong, Moderate, Limited, Disputed, and Refuted.

This GCEP will analyze gene:disease relationships for ARVC; the ARVC gene list includes Cypher/ZASP *(LDB3), RYR2, DSP, CDH2, JUP, DES, DSC2, DSG2, LMNA, CTNNA3, PLN, SCN5A, TTN, TMEM43, PKP2,* and *TGFB3*.

Addendum: In December 2018, additional genes were added to this gene list. These genes included the sarcomere genes and newly associated ARVC genes found in the literature: *ACTC1, MYBPC3, MYH7, MYL2, MYL3, TNNI3, TNNT2, TPM1, TNNC1* and *TJP1*.

***

**The Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC) was formed in 2017, and their initial scope of work included 16 genes thought to be potentially associated with ARVC as defined by the 2010 Task Force Criteria. In December 2018 an additional 10 genes were added including the sarcomere genes and several genes with new assertions of ARVC causation. The group completed evaluation of these genes in 2019, and their findings are described in James CA et al; Circ Genom Precis Med. 2021 14: e003273, PMCID: PMC8205996. The Hereditary Cardiovascular GCEP now re-evaluates these curations on a periodic basis, as described in the ClinGen **[**recuration {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:46","data":{"uuid":"d99bc3fe-7286-430c-a522-70f27a9562d2","name":"Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis Spectrum Disorders","description":"
The ALS Gene Curation Expert panel will evaluate the evidence for genes that have been linked to the phenotype of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). This will include the ALS-spectrum phenotypes of progressive muscular atrophy (PMA), primary lateral sclerosis (PLS), and progressive bulbar palsy. Because both upper and lower motor neuron degeneration can occur in other genetic neurodegenerative disease, the panel will largely exclude genes where an occasional patient meets criteria for ALS *in addition* to their more typical features (e.g. *DJ1* in early onset parkinson’s disease; *NOP36* in spinocerebellar ataxia where bulbar atrophy is observed; several of the complicated hereditary spastic paraplegia genes). However, the panel will evaluate genes typically associated with a non-ALS disease under two circumstances: if there are multiple reports of patients with ALS that lack features of the typical disease (e.g. *VCP,* *ATXN2*, or *BSCL2*) or if the typical neurodegenerative disease associated with the gene is frontotemporal dementia (e.g. *CHMP2B, GRN*). The latter exception is due to the very tight link between ALS and FTD. We also plan to include a narrow set of clinically distinct degenerative motor neuron diseases that each has very little genetic heterogeneity at this point: spinobulbar muscular atrophy/Kennedy’s disease; juvenile ALS; juvenile PLS, Fazio-Londe disease and Brown-Vialetto-Van Laere syndrome. The ALS GCEP will not include the spinal muscular atrophies, distal hereditary motor neuropathies, distal SMA, distal CMT, fronto-temporal dementia, multisystemic proteinopathies, or Troyer’s syndrome.

{"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:45","data":{"uuid":"587acc2c-b453-4767-93d3-58a7a7c31403","name":"Aminoacidopathy","description":"The Aminoacidopathy (AA) GCEP will use the ClinGen Gene-Disease Clinical Validity framework to assess clinical validity for genes associated with aminoacidopathies. This process was piloted by the Metabolism work group with the curation of phenylalanine hydroxylase, the cause of phenylketonuria and related disorders. The curation of five genes for the other disorders leading to hyperphenylalaninemia, namely defects in biopterin synthesis and recycling, has been included to expand this to other disorders of amino acid metabolism.

The aminoacidopathies have been divided into **three tiers** based in part on disease frequency and relevance to newborn screening, where having allelic information may have a significant impact on care management.

**Tier 1** will be curated in the first year and includes urea cycle disorders and related conditions, branched chain amino acid defects, combined propiogenic amino acid defects, as well as disorders of tyrosine metabolism.

**Tier 2** will be curated next and includes disorders of leucine, isoleucine, and valine metabolism, cobalamin, serine, and glycine disorders, in addition to disorders of sulfur amino acids.

**Tier 3** includes disorders of proline, lysine, asparagine, and glutamine metabolism, and will be tackled last.

The AA GCEP will work within the established ClinGen framework, including utilizing ClinGen lumping and splitting guidance as appropriate for each gene curated.

**The Aminoacidopathy GCEP was formed in 2018, and their initial scope of work included 48 genes thought to be associated with aminoacidopathies. The group completed evaluation of these genes in 2024, and is finalizing their publication. The General IEM GCEP now re-evaluates these curations on a ClinGen protocol specified periodicity, as described in the [ClinGen recuration {"event_type":"group_checkpoint_event","schema_version":"2.0.1","date":"2026-04-06 17:40:41","data":{"uuid":"ef9914d5-d2f4-4a3a-b5f9-39aa6cf0f96b","name":"Lysosomal Diseases","description":"Our main goal is to curate variants in genes associated with metabolic diseases primarily involving the lysosome, with an emphasis on disorders on the Recommended Universal Screening Panel (RUSP). Initially, we focused the VCEP’s efforts on modification of the ACMG-AMP criteria for interpretation of variants within *GAA.*  Deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase, encoded by *GAA*, causes Pompe disease (OMIM 232300) glycogen storage disease type II; acid maltase deficiency). This expert panel has also completed specifications for *IDUA* and is actively curating variants within *IDUA*. Deficiency of alpha-L-iduronidase, encoded by *IDUA*, causes Mucopolysaccharidosis Type 1 (MPS I : OMIM 607014, 607015, 607016). Variant interpretation for both genes is an important component of diagnostic confirmation for symptomatic patients, guidance and interpretation for individual identified through newborn screening, as well as individuals identified through genomic sequencing pipelines. The VCEP members will collectively decide which gene to develop specifications for next.

*Please note, in February 2023 the Lysosomal Diseases VCEP was renamed from Lysosomal Storage Disorders Deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase, encoded by GAA, causes Pompe disease (glycogen storage disease type II; acid maltase deficiency). Accurate interpretation of variants within GAA is important for confirmation of the diagnosis in symptomatic patients of any age, asymptomatic infants identified by newborn screening, as well as testing for family members.

Curators within the GAA subgroup are: Jenny Goldstein, Dona Kanavy, Jennifer McGlaughon, Shelly Goomber, Yinghong Pan, Xiangwen Chen-Deutsch, Erin Huggins, Eric Miller, and Chen Yang.

This expert panel is also now curating variants within IDUA. Curators within the IDUA subgroup are: Jenny Goldstein, Roberto Mendez, Xiangwen Chen-Deutsch, Amber Stafford, Matthew Ellinwood, Irina St. Louis, Carlos Aschoff, Kristen Brown, Arty Selvanathan, and Melanie Lacaria.

Pathogenic germline variation in *DICER1* gives rise to an autosomal dominant tumor-predisposition disorder that increases the risk for a variety of benign and malignant tumors, as well as multi-nodular goiter and macrocephaly. *DICER1* is one of about 12 known microRNA-processing genes, most of which have no known human phenotype. One known exception is *DROSHA*, which is associated with Wilms tumor.

The *DICER1* and miRNA-Processing Gene Variant Expert Panel will curate clinically relevant *DICER1* variants using the specified classification rules developed by the group. After interpreting variants using these specified guidelines, the group will make their final interpretations publicly available through ClinVar.
